name: 2-Player Small
ref: All Good Things Competitive Scenario Book, 2.1, p.5
description: Smallest 2-player map with 17 Deep Space markers and 26 Home System markers per side. Quick games ideal for introducing players to the system or when time is limited. Non-Player Aliens recommended.
full_text:
This game is good for introducing players to the system. It is also good for experienced players when they have a time constraint.
• Half-sized hexes on the right side of the playing area are NOT playable (exception see below).
• There are 17 Deep Space markers.
• There are 26 Home System markers on each side. This means that Player 2 must choose one of the half-hexes on the right edge of their playing area within their Home System. This half-hex gets one of the System markers and is fully playable during the game.
• Each player places their home planet in any hex in the row of their Home System that is farthest from their opponent.
• Hexes outside of the playing area are NOT playable.

name: 2-Player Standard
ref: All Good Things Competitive Scenario Book, 2.2, p.5
description: Standard 2-player map with 34 Deep Space markers and 26 Home System markers per side. The default competitive experience for two players.
full_text:
• Half-sized hexes on the right side of the playing area are not playable (exception see below).
• There are 34 Deep Space markers.
• There are 26 Home System markers on each side. This means that Player 1 must choose one of the half-hexes on the right edge of the playing area within their Home System. This half-hex gets one of the System markers and is fully playable during the game.
• Each player places their home planet in any hex in the row of their Home System farthest from their opponent.
• Hexes outside of the playing area are not playable.
• It is recommended that you use Non-Player Aliens (MRB 18.0).

name: 2-Player Large
ref: All Good Things Competitive Scenario Book, 2.3, p.5
description: Large 2-player map with 51 Deep Space markers and 26 Home System markers per side. More room for expansion and exploration before contact.
full_text:
• Half-sized hexes on the right side of the playing area are not playable (exception see below).
• There are 51 Deep Space markers.
• There are 26 Home System markers on each side. This means that Player 2 must choose one of the half-hexes on the right edge of the playing area within their Home System. This half-hex gets one of the System markers and is fully playable during the game.
• Each player places their home planet in any hex in the row of their Home System farthest from their opponent.
• Hexes outside of the playing area are not playable.
• Yeah, you might want to research Exploration...

name: 2-Player Extra Large
ref: All Good Things Competitive Scenario Book, 2.4, p.5
description: Extra large 2-player map with 98 Deep Space markers. Each player sets up their 26 Home System markers in one of the regions normally used in a 4-player game. Exploration technology recommended.
full_text:
• Each player sets up their 26 Home System markers in one of the regions normally used in a 4-Player game.
• The rest of the map, including unused home regions, gets Deep Space markers. This will use 98 of them.
• Each player places their home planet in any hex within their Home System that is on the edge of the board.

name: 2-Player "3D"
ref: All Good Things Competitive Scenario Book, 2.5, p.6
description: Each player sets up in a 4-player quadrant with 46 Deep Space markers between them. The two unused home regions have no System markers but remain traversable — ships entering one side exit from any adjacent hex on the other side, simulating a crude 3D shortcut.
full_text:
The title of this scenario promises more than it gives, but you'll see what we mean.
• Each player sets up their 26 Home System markers in one of the regions normally used in a 4-Player game.
• The rest of the map, except unused home regions, gets Deep Space markers. This uses 46 of them.
• Each player places their home planet in any hex within their Home System that is on the edge of the board.
• The two unused Home Systems get no System markers — they are not in play.
• More than not being in play, the unused Home Systems need to be thought of as not being there. A player may move a ship into an unused Home System and come out in any other hex adjacent to that unused Home System. In essence, it is as if all the hexes adjacent to an unused Home System are adjacent to each other.
• It takes only 1 Movement Point to move from one hex adjacent to an unused Home System to any other hex adjacent to that Home System.
• This is an attempt to introduce a 3D element to the game board. Yes, it's crude. Yes, it's simple. But we think it's clever.

name: 2-Player Massive
ref: All Good Things Competitive Scenario Book, 2.6, p.6
description: Massive 2-player map with 98 Deep Space markers. Each player sets up in a 4-player quadrant with 26 Home System markers. Player 1 has 1 extra HS marker, Player 2 has 6 extras placed adjacent to their area.
full_text:
• Each player sets up their 26 Home System markers as suggested by the diagram. There are no guides on the game board to indicate this set up.
• Player 1 will have one Home System marker and Player 2 will have six Home System markers that do not fit within the area indicated by the line. These should be placed in any hex adjacent to that area.
• The rest of the map gets Deep Space markers. This will use 98 of them.
• Each player places their home planet in any hex within their Home System that is on the edge of the board.

name: "I Dare You to Play This"
ref: All Good Things Competitive Scenario Book, 2.7, p.6
description: Enormous 2-player map with 98 Deep Space markers. Each player places 26 Home System markers in the first two rows closest to their side, remaining marker in any adjacent hex. A sprawling, long game.
full_text:
• Each player sets up their 26 Home System markers as suggested by the diagram. There are no guides on the game board to indicate this set up.
• Each player sets up their 26 Home System markers as suggested by the diagram. There are no guides on the game board to indicate this set up.
• The first two rows closest to each player will be filled by 25 of the Home System markers. The remaining marker should be placed in any hex adjacent to that area.
• The rest of the map gets Deep Space markers. This will use 98 of them.
• Each player places their home planet in any hex within their Home System that is on the edge of the board.

name: 2-Player Double Trouble
ref: All Good Things Competitive Scenario Book, 2.8, p.6
description: Each player uses all the pieces of their color plus the Home System markers of another color (50 HS markers total per player). 50 Deep Space markers. Each player can use Colony counters of either of their colors. A resource-rich, expansive game.
full_text:
• Each player uses all the pieces of their color, plus the Home System markers of another color. One non-barren planet and the home planet is removed from each player's additional color.
• The Home System markers of both colors are mixed together and each player sets up these 50 Home System markers as suggested by the diagram. There are no guides on the game board to indicate this set up.
• The first four rows closest to each player will be filled by their 50 Home System markers.
• There are 50 Deep Space markers.
• Each player places their home planet in any hex within their Home System that is on the edge of the board.
• Each player can use the Colony markers of either of their colors.

name: 2-Player Knife Fight
ref: All Good Things Competitive Scenario Book, 2.9, p.7
description: No Deep Space markers — only Home System markers in adjacent quadrants. Both players start with Ship Size 2, Ship Size 3, and Move 2 already researched, plus 4 Scouts and 4 Colony Ships instead of the standard starting forces. Immediate, aggressive conflict.
full_text:
• Setup: All 26 Home System markers are used for both players. However no deep space markers are used. One player sets up his home system markers in one of the four quadrants used in the four player game. The other player sets up his markers in quadrant fashion, but adjacent to the first player's quadrant with no deep space markers between them. The home planets are set up on the "X".
• Starting Ships: Both players start with 4 SCs and 4 Colony Ships instead of 3 of each. Other starting units remain as per 1.1.1.
• Starting Tech: Both players start the game with Ship Size 2, Ship Size 3, and Move 2 already researched.

name: 2-Player Galactic Capitol
ref: All Good Things Competitive Scenario Book, 2.10, p.7
description: Standard-sized 2-player map with the Galactic Capitol in the center hex, surrounded by Deep Space. 43 Deep Space markers including the GC. 26 Home System markers per side. Can also be played without the Galactic Capitol.
full_text:
• A 2-player game with five rows of Deep Space in between and the Galactic Capitol in the center hex labeled "GC" (MRB 28.1).
• Including the Galactic Capitol there are a total of 43 Deep Space markers.
• There are 26 Home System markers on each side. This means that both players must choose one of the half-hexes on the right edge of their playing area within their Home System. This half-hex gets one of the System markers and is fully playable during the game.
• This map can also be played without the Galactic Capitol.

name: 2-Player — Single Talon Map
ref: All Good Things Competitive Scenario Book, 2.11, p.7
description: Uses the map board from the game Talon (GMT Games). Two empires set up on opposite sides with 118 Deep Space markers between them. Partial hexes are not used.
full_text:
• This scenario requires the use of the map board from the game Talon, also available separately through GMT Games. Two Empires set up on the opposite sides of the board as shown.
• There are 118 Deep Space markers.
• Partial hexes are not used.

name: 2-Player — Both Talon Maps
ref: All Good Things Competitive Scenario Book, 2.12, p.8
description: Uses both map boards from Talon placed together. Two empires on opposite sides with 198 Deep Space markers. Partial hexes are not used. An enormous playing field.
full_text:
• This scenario requires the use of both map boards from the game Talon, also available through GMT Games. The two boards are placed as above, with two Empires set up on the opposite sides of the map as shown.
• Each player's Homeworld is placed in a hex marked with an "H." Since there are 30 Home System hexes, each player will have to use 4 of the new Home System markers (marked with a "v" on the front side) at random.
• There are 198 Deep Space markers.
• Partial hexes are not used.

name: 2-Player — "The Belt" (Talon Map)
ref: All Good Things Competitive Scenario Book, 2.13, p.8
description: Uses the Talon map with a red Belt region separating two empires. 96 Deep Space markers. Home System markers from a different empire are mixed into the Belt. All Belt and Deep Space planets yield an Alien Technology Card when colonized. Non-Player Aliens on all non-Barren planets. Additional victory condition: having a Colony on 6 Belt planets. Doomsday Machines and Warp Points not recommended.
full_text:
2.13.1 Setup
• This scenario requires the use of the map board from the game Talon, also available separately through GMT Games. Two Empires set up on the opposite sides of the board as shown. Each player's Homeworld is placed in a hex marked with an "H." The Home System markers from a different Empire are placed in the red belt between the two Empires. Remove the Homeworld, one non-Barren Planet, one Asteroid counter, and one Nebula before mixing them.
• There are 96 Deep Space markers.

2.13.2 Special Rules
• Partial hexes are not used.
• Do not play with the Amazing Diplomats Empire Advantage.
• Non-Player Aliens are on all Deep Space and Belt planets, including those that are not Barren. It is possible that you will run out of NPAs in this scenario. If that happens, use a spare counter to mark which planets still have NPAs and how many and draw specific ships when they are attacked.
• All planets in Deep Space and the Belt give an Alien Technology Card when colonized (MRB 11.0). If you run out of Alien Technology Cards, reshuffle the discards to form a new draw pile.
• It is recommended that Doomsday Machines (MRB 29.0) and Warp Points (MRB 28.2) are NOT used.

2.13.3 Victory Conditions: In addition to the normal victory conditions, a player can also win by having a Colony on six of the belt planets.

Design Note: This is a longer game, not only because of the size of the map, but because any new Colonies in the belt will be vulnerable. It will be harder for one side to take a dominating lead and both sides should end up deeper in the tech tree.

name: 2-Player — "Belt and Void" (Talon Map)
ref: All Good Things Competitive Scenario Book, 2.14, p.8
description: Same as The Belt scenario but Deep Space hexes are replaced by a "Void" zone with strange radiation. Only small ships may enter the Void. Not recommended with Replicators.
full_text:
2.14.1 Setup: Everything is the same as The Belt scenario, except that the Deep Space hexes are referred to as the "Void." Add Deep Space markers to every hex of the Void.

2.14.2 Special Rules
• In the Void, (but not the Belt hexes), there are strange radiation and spatial properties that make it difficult for certain ships to enter.
• Only Decoys, Minesweepers, Scouts, Destroyers, Raiders, Miners, Transports, Titans, and any advanced versions of those ships may enter the Void.
• Fighters and Ground Units that are carried by one of the above vessels (or on a Colony) are also allowed.
• Space Pirates are allowed but captured Non-Player Alien ships are not allowed to move in the Void.
• Bases and Shipyards can be built at Colonies in the Void and can move in the Void if using the On the Move Empire Advantage.
• It is not recommended that this scenario be played with Replicators.

Design Note: Taking a planet and establishing SYs in the belt to build bigger ships is the key to the game. Ground Units will need to be used since Colony Ships cannot enter the Void.

name: 3-Player Standard
ref: All Good Things Competitive Scenario Book, 3.1, p.9
description: Standard 3-player map. Each player sets up their 26 Home System markers in one of the regions normally used in a 3-player game.
full_text:
• Each player sets up their 26 Home System markers in one of the regions normally used in a 3-Player game (denoted by the shading on the game board).
• The rest of the map (including the two hexes "behind" Player 2 and Player 3), gets Deep Space markers. This will use 72 of them.
• Each player places their home planet in the hex marked with an "X" in the diagram above.
• If all players agree, players may place their Homeworlds in any hex of their Home System. Otherwise, they use the hex marked with an "H".

name: 3-Player Variable
ref: All Good Things Competitive Scenario Book, 3.2, p.9
description: Random Homeworld placement using a D20 or numbered draw. All Home System and Deep Space markers are mixed together and randomly placed. Often leads to unbalanced maps where diplomacy becomes key.
full_text:
• Each player takes their Homeworld and places it randomly. A 20-sided die can be used for this. If one is not available, then pieces of paper numbered 1 to 13 can be drawn out of a cup. The first number rolled/drawn is the row in which your Homeworld will be placed and the second number rolled/drawn is the number of hexes counted over in that row. If a number is rolled that is out of range, then the die is re-rolled until one in range is achieved.
• All other System markers, both Home System and Deep Space, are mixed together and randomly placed all over the map.
• This will often lead to unbalanced map layouts. However, for those that love diplomacy, this is all part of the challenge. Those that have weaker positions often gang up on the strong.

name: 3-Player Teams — 2 vs 1
ref: All Good Things Competitive Scenario Book, 3.3, p.9
description: Standard 3-player map played as 2 vs 1. The team of two operates under UNEASY ALLIANCE rules.
full_text:
• Setup is the same as the 3-Player Standard scenario (3.1).
• Mutually agree or determine randomly which two players will be on the same team versus the third player.
• The two teammates play under the rules as if they have an UNEASY ALLIANCE (1.3.1).
• A player is eliminated when their Homeworld is destroyed. The team wins as soon as they destroy the Homeworld of the single player. The single player must eliminate both of the other players in order to win. However, the single player does not get the 30 CP conquering bonus listed in the Multiplayer Long Game Victory Condition (1.1.8).
• Each of the allied players collect income normally, but all of their ships and technology cost 1.5 times normal (round up). That means a Scout would cost 9 instead of 6, a Destroyer would cost 14 instead of 9, and Tactics 1 Technology would cost 23 instead of 15 (for example).
• Maintenance and turn order bid would still be normal cost.

name: 3-Player Teams — 2 vs 1 with Empire Advantages
ref: All Good Things Competitive Scenario Book, 3.4, p.9
description: Same as 3-Player Teams 2 vs 1 but with Empire Advantages. The team pays normal cost. The solo player draws 6 and chooses 4.
full_text:
The setup and rules for this scenario are the same as given in the 3-Player Teams – 2 vs 1 scenario (3.3), with the following exceptions:
• The team of two players pays the normal cost in CP for everything. They do not have to pay 1.5 times the normal amount.
• The team of two players does not get any Empire Advantages (MRB 10.0), but the single player draws six Empire Advantage cards and gets to choose four of them. As normal, these Empire Advantages remain secret until used in such a way that they would be revealed or a Colony is invaded.

name: 3-Player Teams — 2 vs 1 with Replicators
ref: All Good Things Competitive Scenario Book, 3.5, p.9
description: Same as 3-Player Teams 2 vs 1. Replicator on the solo side. Replicator Colonies start at 1 CP.
full_text:
• Setup is the same as the 3-Player Teams - 2 vs 1 scenario (3.3).
• Mutually agree or determine trandomly which two players will be on the same team versus the third player.
• The two teammates operate under an UNEASY ALLIANCE (1.3.1).
• In this game the Replicator's Colonies start at 1 CP instead of Start. They can still be bombarded back down to Start and will then grow to 1 CP – 3 CP – Full.
• A player is eliminated when their Homeworld is destroyed. The team wins as soon as they destroy the Replicator player's Homeworld. The Replicator player must eliminate both of the other players in order to win. However, the Replicator player does not get the 30 CP conquering bonus listed in the Multiplayer Long Game Victory Condition (1.1.8.)

name: 4-Player Standard
ref: All Good Things Competitive Scenario Book, 4.1, p.10
description: Standard 4-player map. Each player sets up 26 Home System markers in one of the four outlined quadrants. 46 Deep Space markers fill the center.
full_text:
• Each player sets up their 26 Home System markers in one of the regions normally used in a 4-Player game (denoted by the outlined sections on the game board).
• The rest of the map gets Deep Space markers. This will use 46 of them.
• Each player places their home planet in any hex within their Home System that is on the edge of the board.

name: 4-Player Variable
ref: All Good Things Competitive Scenario Book, 4.2, p.10
description: Same as the Variable 3-Player Map. Random Homeworld placement with all markers mixed and placed randomly.
full_text: • Setup is the same as the Variable 3-Player Map (3.2).

name: 4-Player Team Play
ref: All Good Things Competitive Scenario Book, 4.3, p.10
description: Standard 4-player map played as 2 vs 2. Teams determined randomly. Players choose UNEASY ALLIANCE or BLOOD BROTHERS.
full_text:
• Set up is the same as the Standard 4-Player Map (4.1).
• Mutually agree or determine randomly what the teams will be.
• Teammates can be diagonally across from each other or next to each other depending on personal preference.
• Players must decide to play UNEASY ALLIANCE or BLOOD BROTHERS.
• UNEASY ALLIANCE: Teammates may never stack ships of any kind in the same hex and may not use each other's MS Pipelines.
• BLOOD BROTHERS: Teammates execute their turns at the same time, may stack together, may attack together, and may use each other's MS Pipelines for movement. However, they may not share research or CPs.
• In either case, the teammates win or lose the game together.

name: 4-Player Teams with Empire Advantages
ref: All Good Things Competitive Scenario Book, 4.4, p.10
description: Same as 4-Player Team Play. All players draw Empire Advantages as per the normal method.
full_text:
• Setup is the same as 4-Player Team Play (4.3).
• All players draw Empire Advantages as per either normal method (MRB 10.0).

name: 4-Player Teams — 3 vs 1
ref: All Good Things Competitive Scenario Book, 4.5, p.10
description: Standard 4-player map played as 3 vs 1. The three-player team operates as BLOOD BROTHERS. Solo player gets double income and extra Empire Advantages.
full_text:
The set up and rules for this scenario are the same as given in the 4-Player Team Play scenario (4.3), with the following exceptions:
• Instead of 2 vs 2, this scenario is 3 vs 1.
• The three players that are on the same team are operating under BLOOD BROTHERS (1.3.2).
• The player on their own receives double the normal CP income from all colonies except their home planet. Example: If a player has a 30 CP Home Planet, two 3 CP colonies, one of which was connected by an MS Pipeline, the player would get 43 CP.
• The single player draws three Empire Advantage cards (MRB 10.0) and picks one. The three-player team draws a pool of five Empire Advantage cards and each player will get to pick one of them.
• Each of the players on the three-player team starts with the technologies Terraforming 1 and Scanners 1.
• The player on their own must destroy all three of the enemy home worlds.

WARNING: This has the possibility of becoming a long scenario.

Design Note: This has the possibility of becoming a long scenario.

name: 4-Player Teams — 3 vs 1 with Replicators
ref: All Good Things Competitive Scenario Book, 4.6, p.10
description: Same as 4-Player Teams 3 vs 1. The Replicator player must be on the team with 3 players.
full_text:
The setup and rules for this scenario are the same as given in the 4-Player Teams – 3 vs 1 scenario (4.5), with the following exceptions:
• The Replicator player must be on the team with 3 players.
• The Replicator player draws two Empire Advantages (MRB 10.0) and keeps one while the other two players on their team draw three Empire Advantages total and each keep one.

name: 4-Player Deep Space
ref: All Good Things Competitive Scenario Book, 4.7, p.10
description: Smaller Home Systems (14 markers per player) with 94 Deep Space markers. A Deep Space-heavy map with emphasis on exploration and expansion.
full_text:
• There are 14 Home System Markers used by each player. From their Home System markers, each player selects their Homeworld, five planets, one Barren Planet, and seven Mineral counters.
• Each player places their Homeworld in a hex marked with an "H" and their remaining 13 Home System markers are mixed and placed within 3 hexes of the home planet.
• The rest of the hexes get Deep Space markers — 94 will be used.

name: 4-Player Three Row Deep Space
ref: All Good Things Competitive Scenario Book, 4.8, p.11
description: 25 Home System markers per player (minus one 5-Mineral). 50 Deep Space markers between the quadrants. Bottom row not used.
full_text:
• There are 25 Home System markers used by each player. From their Home System markers, each player removes one 5-Mineral marker, then places their Homeworld in a hex marked with an "H" then the remaining 24 Home System markers for each player are mixed and placed within the boundaries shown in the diagram.
• The row of hexes at the bottom of the map do not get markers and are not used.
• There are 50 Deep Space markers.

name: 4-Player Four Row Deep Space
ref: All Good Things Competitive Scenario Book, 4.9, p.11
description: Same as Three Row Deep Space but with 4 rows of Deep Space. 24 Home System markers per player.
full_text:
• There are 25 Home System markers used by each player. From their Home System markers, each player removes one 5-Mineral marker, then places their Homeworld in a hex marked with an "H" then the remaining 24 Home System markers for each player are mixed and placed within the boundaries.
• There are 38 Deep Space markers.

name: 4-Player Two Maps
ref: All Good Things Competitive Scenario Book, 4.10, p.11
description: Requires two copies of Space Empires 4X played on two boards connected by their long edges. 188 Deep Space markers.
full_text:
• This scenario requires two copies of Space Empires 4X and is played on two boards. Twelve blank hex tiles are used to connect the two boards on their long edges. If terrain is revealed during the game in one of the blank hex tile spaces, the blank is removed and replaced with the appropriate Terrain tile (MRB 6.10).
• Each player places their Homeworld in a hex marked with an "H." Since there are 31 Home System hexes, each player will have to use 5 of the new Home system markers (marked with a "v" on the front side) at random.
• There are 188 Deep Space markers.

name: 4-Player Two Maps — Corners
ref: All Good Things Competitive Scenario Book, 4.11, p.12
description: Uses the Talon map. Each player places their Homeworld in a corner. 25 Home System markers per player. 66 Deep Space markers. Partial hexes not used.
full_text:
• This scenario requires the use of the map board from the game Talon, also available separately through GMT Games. Each player's Homeworld is placed in a hex marked with an "H." The remaining 25 Home System markers for each player are mixed and placed within the boundaries shown in the diagram above.
• The rest of the hexes get Deep Space markers. This will use 66 of them.
• Partial hexes are not used.

name: 4-Player Large (Talon Map)
ref: All Good Things Competitive Scenario Book, 4.12, p.12
description: Uses the Talon map with players on opposite edges. A large 4-player map on a single Talon board.
full_text: • Setup is a slightly modified version of the 4-Player Two Maps scenario (4.10) — only add 4 new Home system markers instead of 5.

name: 4-Player — Both Talon Maps (Long Edge)
ref: All Good Things Competitive Scenario Book, 4.13, p.12
description: Two Talon boards placed long edge to long edge. 25 Home System markers per player. 154 Deep Space markers. Partial hexes not used.
full_text:
• This scenario requires the use of both map boards from the game Talon, also available through GMT Games. The two boards are arranged as above. Each player places their Homeworld in a hex marked with an "H" then the remaining 25 Home System markers for each player are mixed and placed within the boundaries shown in the diagram above.
• There are 154 Deep Space markers.
• Partial hexes are not used.

name: 4-Player — Both Talon Maps (Short Edge)
ref: All Good Things Competitive Scenario Book, 4.14, p.12
description: Slightly modified version of the Long Edge layout with boards placed short edge to short edge.
full_text: • Setup is a slightly modified version of the 4-Player – Both Talon Maps (Long Edge) scenario (4.13).

name: 4-Player — Both Talon Maps (with Gaps)
ref: All Good Things Competitive Scenario Book, 4.15, p.12
description: Slightly modified version of the Long Edge layout with gaps between player areas.
full_text: • Setup is a slightly modified version of the 4-Player – Both Talon Maps (Long Edge) scenario (4.13).

name: 5-Player Deep Space
ref: All Good Things Competitive Scenario Book, 5.1, p.13
description: 5-player map with 11 Home System markers per player. 95 Deep Space markers.
full_text:
• There are 11 Home System markers used by each player. From their Home System markers, each player selects their Homeworld, five planets, one Barren planet, and five Mineral counters.
• A Replicator player uses their Homeworld, four planets, one Barren planet, four 10-Mineral counters, and one 5-Mineral counter.
• Each player places their Homeworld in a hex marked with an "H" and their remaining 10 System markers are mixed and placed within two hexes of the Homeworld.
• There are 95 Deep Space markers.

name: 5-Player Tight
ref: All Good Things Competitive Scenario Book, 5.2, p.13
description: 5-Player Deep Space map with 19 Home System markers per player. 55 Deep Space markers. Tight quarters.
full_text:
• This uses the same map as the 5-Player Deep Space scenario (5.1).
• There are 19 Home System markers used by each player. From their Home System markers, each player selects their Homeworld, eight planets, one Asteroid, one Nebula, and eight Mineral counters.
• A Replicator player uses their Homeworld, seven planets, one Barren planet, two Asteroids, two Nebulae, four 10-Mineral counters, and two 5-Mineral counters.
• Each player places their Homeworld in a hex marked with an "H" then the remaining 18 System markers are mixed and placed within three hexes of the Homeworld.
• There are 55 Deep Space markers. When setting up this map, at least one hex of Deep Space must be between each player's Home System hexes.
• There may be a turn or two where you will need to use spare numeral markers to keep track of the Colony growth for all five players.

name: 5-Player Teams — 3 vs 2
ref: All Good Things Competitive Scenario Book, 5.3, p.13
description: Uses the 5-Player Tight map and setup. Replicator must be on the 3-player side.
full_text:
• Use the same map and setup as the 5-Player Tight scenario (5.2).
• The Replicator Player must be part of the 3-player side.
• The 3-Player side draws no Empire Advantages (MRB 10.0).
• The 2-Player team draws six Empire Advantages and decides together which four they will use. Each player gets the benefit of only two of the cards.
• Both teams operate as an UNEASY ALLIANCE (1.3.1).

name: 6-Player Two Row Deep Space
ref: All Good Things Competitive Scenario Book, 6.1, p.13
description: 6 players arranged in two rows of 3. Tight, aggressive map with 25 Deep Space markers.
full_text:
• There are 21 Home System markers used by each player, except player 5, which only uses 20 Home System markers.
• From their Home System markers, each player removes two 5-Minerals, one non-barren planet, one Asteroid, and one Nebula. Player 5 removes two Asteroids instead of one.
• Each player places their Homeworld in a hex marked with an "H" then the remaining 20 Home System markers (19 for player 5) for each player are mixed and placed within the boundaries shown in the diagram.
• There are 25 Deep Space markers.

name: 6-Player Two Maps
ref: All Good Things Competitive Scenario Book, 6.2, p.14
description: Requires two copies of Space Empires 4X. Two boards connected by their long edges. 102 Deep Space markers. Partial hexes not used.
full_text:
• This scenario requires two copies of Space Empires 4X and is played on two boards. Twelve blank hex tiles are used to connect the two boards on their long edges. If terrain is revealed during the game in one of the blank hex tile spaces, the blank is removed and replaced with the appropriate Terrain tile (MRB 6.10).
• Twelve blank hex tiles are used to connect the two boards on their long edges.
• Each player places their Homeworld in a hex marked with an "H" then the remaining 25 Home System markers for each player are mixed and placed within the boundaries shown in the diagram above.
• There are 102 Deep Space markers.
• Partial hexes are not used.

name: 6-Player — Both Talon Maps
ref: All Good Things Competitive Scenario Book, 6.3, p.14
description: Uses both Talon map boards. 3 players on each side. 102 Deep Space markers. Partial hexes not used.
full_text:
• This scenario requires the use of both map boards from the game Talon, also available separately through GMT Games. The two boards are arranged as above, with each player's Homeworld placed in a hex marked with an "H." The remaining 25 Home System markers for each player are mixed and placed within the boundaries shown in the diagram above.
• There are 102 Deep Space markers.
• Partial hexes are not used.

name: 3-Player Quick Conquest
ref: All Good Things Competitive Scenario Book, 7.2, p.14
description: Fast-paced 3-player conquest scenario. No Mines or Minesweepers. Uses TECHNOLOGY HEAD START Scenario Card. First to destroy a Homeworld wins.
full_text:
Although the Multiplayer Short Game Victory Condition (using Colony Points, 1.1.7) has been popular for providing a shorter, non-team multiplayer game, the point of this game has always been conquest. These scenarios are meant to provide a quicker multiplayer game with conquest as the victory condition.

7.1 Setup
• Each player places their Homeworld in a hex marked with an "H" in the diagrams below.
• No Mines or Minesweepers may be researched or used.
• Use the 30 CP Homeworld counters.
• Use the TECHNOLOGY HEAD START Scenario Card (see the Card Manifest) in addition to any other Scenario Cards.
• The first player to destroy a Homeworld is the winner.
• These scenarios can also be played with teams.

name: 4-Player Quick Conquest
ref: All Good Things Competitive Scenario Book, 7.3, p.15
description: Fast-paced 4-player conquest scenario. Same rules as 3-Player Quick Conquest.
full_text: Same rules as 3-Player Quick Conquest (7.2) but using the 4-Player map layout. Each player places their Homeworld in a hex marked with an "H."

name: Epic Scenarios for 2-8 Players
ref: All Good Things Competitive Scenario Book, 8.0, p.15
description: Framework for playing with 2 to 8 players across two game boards connected by Warp Points.
full_text:
• These scenarios require two copies of the game and are played on two boards (see 4.10 for how to lay out two boards).
• These are meant to enable a game with more than four players, but any of the scenario set ups can be used for an epic scenario. The possibilities are numerous.
  - If six players are in the game, using the 3-Player Standard set up on both boards makes sense.
  - With seven players, using the 3-Player Standard setup on one board and the 4-Player Standard set up on the other would work.
  - A 2-Player game can be played where each player controls an empire on both maps.
  - A 4-Player game could be played with two players on each map using the "I Dare You to Play This" set ups on both maps (in this case, it is recommended that each player use all the Colony Ships of his color from both games).
  - An 8-Player game could be played with a random map set up (3.2 or 4.2).
• Warp Points: Warp Points (MRB 28.2) are the connection between the boards. All six Warp Point 1s (three on each board) are considered connected. The same with all six Warp Point 2s. Players should agree on the number of Warp Points used on each map. If the set up on a board requires less than the full complement of Deep Space System markers, then the number of markers needed must be counted out and the Warp Points must be among them before they are randomly placed on the board.
• If more than four players are playing, then some of the same color playing pieces would have to be used. There might be two green empires, for example. Empires of the same color should obviously be on separate maps. They are differentiated from each other by being on separate maps. When a unit crosses over maps, it should be marked with a spare token.
• In an epic scenario, when one player conquers another, they add all the conquered player's pieces to their build pool.

name: 2-Player Planetary Gates
ref: All Good Things Competitive Scenario Book, 9.2, p.16
description: 2-player map with pre-placed Barren Planets and Planetary Gates connecting all planets within 3 hexes.
full_text:
9.1 Special Rules
9.1.1 Planetary Gates: Each planet is connected to any other planet within three hexes by means of a Planetary Gate. Ground Units (MRB 21.0) on one planet may move to any other planet within three hexes via this Planetary Gate. That is considered their entire move that turn. The AIR SUPPORT Alien Technology card does not enable Transports to use the Planetary Gates. Colonies built on asteroids by the Industrious Race are not connected by Planetary Gates.

9.1.2 Barren Planets: Barren planets may be pre-placed in deep space between home systems so that it increases the likelihood that players are connected by Planetary Gates. In the diagrams below, barren planets are placed on the hexes marked with a "B." These are suggestions for the 2-player, 3-player, and 4-player game. However, players should feel free to play without pre-placement of planets or come up with some other mutually agreed upon layout. Players may also decide to remove all Barren Planets from the remaining Deep Space System markers to limit extra connectivity.

9.1.3 Combat through Gates: If Ground Units are moved by a Planetary Gate and the destination planet is colonized by another player or is a non-player alien (MRB 18.0), ground combat occurs normally. Regardless of the ground combat technology of the invading player, attacking units do not get to attack in the first round. The invasion and the result are handled exactly the same as if the planet was invaded from space.

9.1.4 Retreats: Ground Units may retreat through gates, but only to friendly planets within three hexes that are free of enemy units. They may retreat before bombardment or during ground combat when it is their turn to fire, but not during the first round. Fighters may not retreat by Planetary Gates.

9.1.5 Alien Planets: An NPA planet may be captured by invading through a Planetary Gate. In that case, any alien ships still in orbit are removed from the game.

9.1.6 Gates and Fighters: Fighters (MRB 15.2) may move between planets like Ground Units, but only between friendly Colonies.

9.1.7 Colony Ships: When a Colony Ship is purchased, it may be purchased as a non-spacefaring Colony Ship. It still costs the same, but it can be placed, without the use of a SY, on any Colony that produced 5 CP in that Economic Phase. It should be placed ship-side-up on top of the existing Colony. A non-spacefaring Colony Ship can never move into space but may travel through Planetary Gates like ground units. It must colonize the first uncolonized planet that it moves to. The ship is left on the uncolonized planet and is flipped in the Economic Phase just as it would be if it was a normal Colony Ship.

Similar to space combat, the Colony Ship may enter a hex with enemy units only when accompanied by friendly ground units, may not retreat, and is destroyed if the enemy wins the combat.

Design Note: Just when you think you don't need ships any more, that fleet shows up and starts wasting your Colonies from space. Then when you let your guard down on the ground, an invasion force shows up in your Home System and starts spreading from planet to planet.

name: 3-Player Planetary Gates
ref: All Good Things Competitive Scenario Book, 9.3, p.16
description: 3-player version of Planetary Gates with Barren Planets pre-placed between all three empires.
full_text: Same Planetary Gate rules as the 2-Player version (9.1). 3-player map with Barren Planets pre-placed on hexes marked "B" between all three empires.

name: 4-Player Planetary Gates
ref: All Good Things Competitive Scenario Book, 9.4, p.16
description: 4-player version of Planetary Gates with Barren Planets pre-placed in the center of the map.
full_text: Same Planetary Gate rules as the 2-Player version (9.1). 4-player map with Barren Planets pre-placed on hexes marked "B" in the center and between empires.

name: 2-Player Space Amoebas
ref: All Good Things Competitive Scenario Book, 10.9, p.19
description: Standard 2-player map with Space Amoebas pre-placed between the two empires.
full_text:
10.0 Space Amoeba Scenarios
Giant Space Amoebas can be added to many of the competitive scenarios in this book. During the game, the Space Amoebas will multiply and attempt to destroy the human players. There are similar rules for Space Amoebas included in the Solo/Co-Op Scenario Book (SSB 3.0).

10.1 Setup
• Space Amoebas may be pre-placed in Deep Space between the Home Systems. No Deep Space marker is placed in a hex with an Amoeba.
• In the diagrams to follow, Space Amoebas are placed on the hexes marked with an "S" as suggestions. Players should feel free to play with some other mutually agreed upon layout.
• All Space Amoebas are of one type (unless the players agree upon multiple types). A Space Amoeba counter of any of the three included types may be used.

10.2 Special Rules
• Unlike the solitary Space Amoeba scenario there are no rule restrictions (i.e., Titans, Ground Combat, Facilities, etc. can be used).
• Unlike the solitary Space Amoeba scenario players must pay maintenance as usual.
• Unlike the solitary Space Amoeba scenario Barren Planets do have Non-Player Aliens (MRB 18.0) on them.
• Minesweepers (SW) represent Science Vessels (SV) and pull double duty. Minesweeper technology also counts as Science and must be researched to produce hybrid Minesweeper/Science Vessels. A Minesweeper counter may both research Amoebas and sweep Mines.
• Only the first two levels of Minesweeper technology count as Science technology. Minesweeper 3 does not provide additional research bonus.
• If a player is eliminated by an Amoeba, play continues for remaining players.

10.3 Researching Space Amoebas
• Until a player has researched the Amoebas, they cannot be attacked and will destroy all that player's ships (except SVs) in the same hex.
• Unlike the solitaire scenario, a player is not required to lose a ship before researching.
• Each player must research individually.
• Research Points carry over from turn to turn.
• Each turn an SV is in a hex with an Amoeba, roll dice:
  1-2: Two Research Points gained.
  3-5: One Research Point gained.
  6-8: Nothing.
  9-10: SV destroyed.
• Level 1 SVs roll one die. Level 2 SVs roll two dice, take the best.
• Level 2 can't be acquired until Level 1 SV has researched an Amoeba.
• Once a player has 10 Research Points, they may attack any Amoeba for the rest of the game.
• After 10 RP, Amoebas no longer automatically destroy ships. Combat ensues as normal. Science Vessels never participate in combat with an Amoeba.

10.4 Space Amoeba Strength
After 10 RP, the player has figured out how to kill the Amoebas. No retrofit needed (MRB 9.11.3).

10.4.1 Determine Amoeba Strength: The first player to gather 10 RP rolls on the Amoeba Strength Table. This result applies to all players but should not be revealed until others complete their own research.

10.5 Amoeba Strength Table
Roll 1: C7-1-1
Roll 2: D6-2-2
Roll 3: A7-2-2. Also vulnerable to fighters.
Roll 4: A7-2-2. Becomes A5-1-2 when faced by ONLY Raiders.
Roll 5: B5-1-2. Two attacks each round. Each may lead to Chain Attack (10.6.3).
Roll 6-7: A7-2-2. If 3+ different ship types attacking, all get +1 Attack.
Roll 8-10: Can only be killed by detonating a ship inside. CA/BC/BB/DN/Titan = auto-destroy. DD/MB = destroyed on 1-8. SC = destroyed on 1-4. Ship attempting this is always destroyed. If Amoeba survives, other ships in hex are destroyed.

10.6 Amoeba Properties
• All Amoebas have Tactics Level 2.
• All Amoebas are immune to Cloaking and Fighters unless otherwise specified.
• Ships never get Fleet Size Bonus (MRB 5.1.3) against Amoebas.

10.6.1 Amoeba Target Priority: Roll a die. 1-5 = attack smallest ship (by CP). 6-10 = attack largest. Flagships considered highest CP. Ties chosen by player.

10.6.2 Amoeba Attack Procedure: Rolls one die, comparing Attack Strength to Defense Strength (including tech). Hull Size 2 ships get +1 Defense. Hull Size 3+ get +2 Defense. Successful attack = swallowed regardless of size. Roll of 1 always succeeds.

10.6.3 Chain Attacks: If Amoeba swallows a ship and rolls 2 or less than needed, it immediately attacks again. Can happen any number of times per round.

10.6.4 Amoeba Healing: Healed same way as ships after combat.

10.7 Spreading of Space Amoebas
Amoebas never move. They spread at the start of the Economic Phase before purchases.

10.7.1 Spread Procedure:
• Do not start spreading until Economic Phase 7 (or by mutual agreement).
• Only one Amoeba per hex.
• Each Amoeba spreads one time per Economic Phase.
• In turn order, each player picks an Amoeba and chooses its spread hex.
• If it can spread onto a planet, it must.
• Newly placed Amoebas do not spread that phase.
• Amoebas never spread outside the scenario map area.
• Space Amoebas will not spread into a DM hex and DMs will not enter Amoeba hexes.
• Not limited by counter mix. Use substitutions.

10.7.2 Contact with Ships: If ships in hex where Amoeba spreads, special combat round occurs immediately. If player has not gathered 10 RP, all non-SV ships destroyed. Amoebas automatically destroy NPA ships.

10.7.3 Contact with System Markers: Amoeba removes System marker in any hex it spreads to (after defeating ships). Will not spread into Black Holes or Supernovas. Skip Folds in Space. Otherwise unaffected by System markers.

10.8 Space Amoebas and Mines
Mines hinder spreading but don't affect Amoebas like ships.

10.8.1 Procedure: If only a Mine (or Mine + SV) in same hex, no combat. Mine removed in Economic Phase, Amoeba doesn't spread that phase.

10.8.2 Contact with Mines: Amoebas may spread onto Mines normally. Mines remain in hex until next Economic Phase when they may stop spreading.

10.8.3 Adapting to Mines: Once per Economic Phase an Amoeba doesn't spread due to a Mine, roll a die. On 9-10, Amoebas are no longer prevented from spreading by Mines in future phases. Place "No Mine" counter as reminder.

10.9 2-Player Space Amoebas
Space Amoebas are placed on the hexes marked with an "S" on the Large 2-Player Map.
