name: King of the Warp Point
ref: All Good Things Competitive Scenario Book, 11.1, p.20
description: Alternate victory scenario. Players score VPs by having Science Vessels at Warp Points each Economic Phase.
full_text:
The Warp Points are the key to victory in this scenario. They represent access to vast scientific treasures and ancient civilizations. By studying them, players unlock knowledge that will lead them to make galaxy changing advances.

11.1.1 Setup: Before the Deep Space markers are placed on the map, the Warp Points are taken out of the mix. Depending on the scenario, a number of Warp Points are placed in mutually agreed upon hexes in the Deep Space portion of the map. Some guidelines on Warp Point placement:
• It is strongly suggested that you use either:
  - One Warp Point, or
  - One less Warp Point than the number of players, or
  - One more Warp Point than the number of players.
• All of the Warp Points can be placed centrally, or one could be placed to the advantage of each player with an extra one in the middle. There are a lot of options, and each will change the game.

Design Note: Each of the above will make for a very different and interesting game — is it important to hold one Warp Point or do I need to hold several?

If the players cannot agree on the placement of the Warp Points, then one player should place all the Warp Points while the other players choose their starting positions after he does.

Unused Warp Points should be placed to the side and not mixed in with the rest of the Deep Space Markers.

11.1.2 Warp Point Properties: None of the Warp Points in this scenario are connected. Their only purpose is to provide VPs.

11.1.3 Minesweepers: These ships have a dual function in this scenario. They not only sweep Mines, but also act as Science Vessels. You must still research Mine Sweeping Technology to build SWs (MRB 17.2).

11.1.4 Game Length: Players should decide at the start of the game how many Economic Phases they want the game to last. The game ends after that Economic Phase. 10-12 Economic turns are good for a shorter game, depending on the scenario. This scenario also works with a longer, 15-20 Economic Phase game, although players in that one will have to be careful not to become too focused on the Warp Points as it will become more and more likely that someone will launch a game winning attack against a Homeworld.

11.1.5 Victory Points: Players score 1 VP during the Economic Phase for each Warp Point that shares a hex with one or more of their Science Vessels in the same hex as a Warp Point. All Science Vessels must be revealed during the Economic Phase. The player with the most VPs at the end of the agreed upon number of Economic Phases is the winner. In the event of a tie, after the purchases for the Economic Phase are completed, the players add up the CP value of all combat capable ships they have on the board and the winner is the player whose fleet adds up to the most CP. In the event of another tie, the tied players share the win.

11.1.6 Alternate Point Scoring: As a twist to this scenario, players can agree that they will score 1 VP per Science Vessel at a Warp Point in the Economic Phase. This will change the scenario strategy significantly (weighing combat capability versus number of Science Vessels is tough) and it is recommended that you play this version only with one Warp Point.

name: Grab the Artifacts
ref: All Good Things Competitive Scenario Book, 11.2, p.20
description: Alternate victory scenario. Ancient artifacts in Deep Space must be gathered by Miners and delivered for VPs.
full_text:
Ancient artifacts of great value litter Deep Space and players must gather and study them.

11.2.1 Setup: Extra pieces or counters will be needed for this scenario (pieces from an old Risk game will work well). One of these extra pieces is placed in every Deep Space hex (along with the exploration marker). These will represent the ancient artifacts.

11.2.2 Exploring Artifacts: After exploration, artifacts remain in the hex even if the System marker is removed (Danger!, etc.). Exception: Artifacts in Supernovas (MRB 6.5), Folds In Space (MRB 25.2), and Quantum Filaments (MRB 25.5) are removed.

11.2.3 Moving Artifacts: Artifacts may only be moved by Miners. Place the artifact on the Miner counter when a Miner picks it up. They may pick up an artifact at any point during their move. The following rules apply to moving artifacts:
• Each Miner may carry only one artifact at a time and may not carry minerals or Space Wrecks at the same time.
• Artifacts being towed by Miners are destroyed if the Miner is destroyed.
• Miners may drop off artifacts at their Homeworld or at a Colony that is connected by MS Pipelines to the Homeworld (it is assumed a civilian ship is able to transport the artifact from there). Miners may not drop off artifacts at Colonies that are not yet connected in this way.

11.2.4 Victory Conditions: Each artifact dropped off is worth 1 VP. The game ends immediately the instant it is dropped off (even in the middle of a turn) once someone has scored enough VPs that there is no possible way that their total may be passed by another player given the artifacts remaining on the board. In the event of a tie, the person who achieved their Victory Point total first is declared the winner.

name: Standing Ships Variant
ref: All Good Things Competitive Scenario Book, 12.1, p.21
description: Physical variant using plastic stands for unrevealed ships.
full_text:
The following variants add the use of plastic stands which either provide more access to information or add an additional element of hidden information to the game.

• Unrevealed ships are not placed face down as in the normal game. Instead, ships are placed upright in stands facing the owning player.
• The number of ships in each group is tracked on the Ship Technology Sheet.
• When a group is revealed, it is placed face up and a numeral marker placed underneath.
• Groups still have a limit of six ships.

name: 2-Player Hidden Exploration Variant
ref: All Good Things Competitive Scenario Book, 12.2, p.21
description: Physical variant using stands for System markers. Deep Space markers must be explored by both sides.
full_text:
12.2.1 Changes to Exploration
• The map is set up normally, with face down System markers.
• Deep Space markers must be explored by both sides. When a Deep Space System marker is explored or would be returned face up with Exploration technology (MRB 9.8) for the first time, it is not flipped face up and it may not be left face down. Instead, it is placed upright in a stand facing the exploring player. Some markers will be facing one player and some the other, depending on who explored it. If a marker is explored by the other player, it is then removed from the stand and placed face up. All markers that have been explored by both players will be face up on the board.
• When a Deep Space System marker is explored by a ship for the first time, a Decoy (MRB 8.3) may be added to the hex. Players may scuttle another decoy during the exploration phase to accommodate this. After looking at the System marker and placing it in a stand, the player removes either the Decoy or the exploring ship. There will be one marker left in the hex, but the other player will not know whether it is a ship or a Decoy and therefore will be less likely to know if the Exploration went well.
  - If the System marker indicates that the hex is safe, the player removes the Decoy.
  - If the System marker indicates the ship's destruction, the player removes the ship.

12.2.2 Special Rules
• Deep Space Black Holes (MRB 6.3) are 100% deadly until explored by both players. If not face up or in a stand, a Deep Space Black Hole will always suck in the first exploring ship(s) (no need to roll the die). When this occurs, the Decoy marker is left behind. Future ships entering the hex roll the die as normal (which will give away that this is a Black Hole).
• Alien planets (MRB 18.0) are also 100% deadly if attacked by a lone ship. The first time an alien planet is discovered and placed in a stand, it is treated like a Danger! marker and the exploring ship is removed without die rolls (leaving the Decoy marker behind). NPA ships are not chosen and placed on the planet until both sides have explored the planet or it is attacked by more than one ship.
• Lost in Space markers (MRB 6.6) are treated like Danger! markers when first explored. Future ships from the same player entering the hex later ignore the effect.
• Space Pirates (MRB 25.7) are turned face up and handled normally.
• Regional Maps (MRB 25.6) are turned face up and handled normally (which could cause adjacent markers to be put in stands).
• Minerals (MRB 6.7) and Space Wrecks (MRB 6.8) are placed in stands. However, if that player sends a Miner into the hex, it can be removed from the stand and picked up normally.
• Supernovas (MRB 6.5) are treated like Danger! markers when first explored. If explored by a ship, the Decoy is left behind in the hex and must be moved out during the next turn. It is possible that this could cause a Decoy to pass through a Supernova and out the other side under this one exception. The player that has explored the Supernova may not send ships into the hex.
• Folds in Space (MRB 25.2) have no effect when first explored, remove the Decoy. Future ships may either enter the hex and ignore the usual effects or move through it. Once a ship moves through it remove the marker from the stand and place it face up.
• Quantum Filaments (MRB 25.5) are treated like Danger! markers when first explored. Future ships from the same player may enter, but must spend an extra movement hex as normal.
• Note that a player may ignore beneficial terrain effects (e.g. Pulsars MRB 25.4) to keep the terrain tile hidden from their opponent.

12.2.3 Game Effects
• This variant creates paths that can be easily traversed by one player (because they've explored it), but not the other. That will at times allow a fleet to more easily retreat or avoid combat.
• Both players are more likely to research Exploration technology because they will not be able to easily use a path made by the other player.
• The cost of suffering a bad exploration result is slightly less because the player will receive a Decoy as a result.
• There will be more Decoys on the board.

name: Doomsday Machines Solitaire (Scenarios #1 & #2)
ref: All Good Things Solo/Co-op Scenario Book, 2.0–2.8, p.4–6
description: An evil race sends Doomsday Machines toward your growing empire. Two map sizes available: Small (Deep Space within 2 hexes of Home System, 3 DMs) and Large (Deep Space within 4 hexes, 3 DMs at higher starting strength). Facilities limited to IC and RC only.
full_text:
2.0 1-Player Doomsday Machine Scenarios
An evil race is sending "Doomsday Machines" towards your growing empire!

2.1 Setup
• There are 26 Home System markers for the player. The 30 CP Homeworld should be placed in the hex with the "H."
• There is no need for a turn order bid. The player always takes their turn before the DMs unless 1.1.10 is used.

2.2 Special Rules
Only the following Advanced/Optional Rules may be used in this scenario:
• MS Pipelines (MRB 13.0).
• Carriers, Fighters, & Point Defense (MRB 15.0).
• Raiders (MRB 16.0).
• Mines & Minesweepers (MRB 17.0).
• Optional Deep Space Discoveries (MRB 28.0).
• Instant Technology Upgrade (MRB 30.0).
• Black Hole Slingshot (MRB 31.0).
• Research Gearing Limits (MRB 32.0).
• Unpredictable Research (MRB 33.0).
• Facilities (IC & RC only, MRB 36.0).

2.2.1 Victory Conditions: The player wins by defeating all of the Doomsday Machines (DMs) sent at them and loses if their Homeworld is destroyed.

2.3 Doomsday Machine Details
Doomsday Machines are massive battle stations capable of destroying entire planets.

2.3.1 Arrival Times: The DMs appear at the end of the Economic Phase specified on the scenario's Doomsday Machine Difficulty Table (2.7, 2.8).

2.3.2 Arrival Locations: Map hexes marked with red numbers represent DM entry points. When a DM arrives, roll a die to determine in which hex it appears.

2.3.3 Movement Guidelines: DMs always move towards the closest player Colony. If two Colonies are equidistant, then a DM will always move toward the Colony that is larger (in CP). If more than one equidistant Colonies are the same size, then the DM will move towards the Colony that brings it closest to the player's Homeworld. If it is still tied, then it is the player's choice. As a DM moves toward a Colony, if two different hexes will put it equidistant from the target Colony, it will move toward the hex that is also closer to the player's Homeworld. Each time a DM moves, its target Colony may change. The player may not reduce a Colony or remove it in order to change the direction of a DM.

2.3.4 Strength and Weaknesses: Each map contains a table indicating what Economic Phase each DM enters play and the DM Rating row to use for that DM's statistics (Table 2.5). When DMs enter play, roll 1d10. The DM appears in the matching red-numbered hex on the scenario map. When that DM is first encountered, its weakness is determined by rolling a die and consulting the Doomsday Machine Weakness Table (2.6).

2.3.5 Movement Rate: DMs always move one hex per turn and may not use Warp Points (MRB 28.2). Unlike in multiplayer games, DMs can move in a hex with an unexplored marker and when it does, do NOT flip the marker.

2.3.6 System Markers and DMs: DMs are unaffected by System markers (see exceptions in 2.3.7 and 2.3.8). A player may not move into a hex with a DM and an unexplored System marker. If you wanted to move there you should have explored it before the DM got there!

2.3.7 Special Combat Conditions: A DM's Attack/Defense Strengths are not affected by Asteroids or Nebulae. DMs are affected by Ion Storms (MRB 25.1.3) and Quasars (MRB 5.8). Unlike multiplayer games, DMs do not destroy Asteroids in solitaire scenarios (MRB 29.4).

2.3.8 Planets and Colonies: If a DM is in a hex with a planet (with or without a Colony), it will destroy that planet. Remove the planet and any Colony from play. The DM must first defeat any defending ships.

2.4 Doomsday Machine Combat Rules
• If a DM moves into a hex with the player's ships, it will engage them in combat.
• Unless specified as a weakness, all DMs are immune to Mines, Fighters (their weaponry is too small to affect them), and Cloaking (they are equipped with the highest level of Scanners).
• The player does not get the Fleet Size Bonus (MRB 5.1.3) for outnumbering a DM.
• A roll of 1 is not an automatic hit on a DM. However, a roll of 1 by a DM is always a hit. A DM that rolls a 10 is always a miss.
• DMs carry damage from combat to combat (they do not automatically heal). However, they do heal two damage instantly every time they destroy a planet (whether or not it has a Colony).
• DMs get more than one attack per round as shown on the table (2.5). All attacks are of the same strength and occur at the same time. A DM is considered to have Tactics 2.
• The player should use common sense when allocating the attacks of a DM. If weak against Fighters, it should target Fighters. If weak against large fleets, it should attack smaller ships or damaged ships in order to get the fleet size under 10, etc. Like with human players, DMs may roll their attacks one at a time and see the result before rolling more attacks.
• A DM never retreats from combat.

2.5 Doomsday Machine Strength
DM Rating 1: Attack D7, Defense 1, Hull Size 6, Attacks 3
DM Rating 2: Attack C7, Defense 1, Hull Size 7, Attacks 4
DM Rating 3: Attack C8, Defense 2, Hull Size 7, Attacks 4
DM Rating 4: Attack C8, Defense 2, Hull Size 8, Attacks 4
DM Rating 5: Attack B9, Defense 2, Hull Size 8, Attacks 5
DM Rating 6: Attack B9, Defense 3, Hull Size 9, Attacks 5
DM Rating 7: Attack B10, Defense 3, Hull Size 9, Attacks 6
DM Rating 8: Attack A10, Defense 3, Hull Size 10, Attacks 6
DM Rating 9: Attack A11, Defense 4, Hull Size 10, Attacks 6
DM Rating 10: Attack A11, Defense 4, Hull Size 11, Attacks 6

2.6 Doomsday Machine Weakness
Die Roll 1-2 — Fighters: Fighters can fire and damage a DM normally.
Die Roll 3-4 — Mines: Each Mine will cause a hit on a DM on a roll of 1-5.
Die Roll 5-6 — Cloaking: Raiders fire as A-Class instead of D-Class in every round and have a +1 Attack Strength bonus in the first round. In addition, this DM gets -2 on its Attack Strength when firing at Raiders.
Die Roll 7-8 — Large Fleets: Player will get the Fleet Size Bonus in any round in which they attack the DM with at least 10 ships (that can hit the DM).
Die Roll 9-10 — None.

2.7 Doomsday Machines – Small (Solitaire Scenario #1)
• Deep Space markers are placed within two hexes of the player's Home System areas, except that a marker is not placed in any of the possible DM entry hexes. The player may not enter these hexes.
• The player will face a total of three DMs. Each enters at the start of the Economic Phase at the Rating according to the table below.
• "#/#" – The first number is the Economic Phase the DM enters. The second number is the DM Rating.
Difficulty Table (Small):
Easy: First DM 7/1, Second DM 9/3, Third DM 11/5
Normal: First DM 7/2, Second DM 9/4, Third DM 10/6
Hard: First DM 6/1, Second DM 8/3, Third DM 10/5
Harder: First DM 6/2, Second DM 8/4, Third DM 9/6
Good Luck!: First DM 5/1, Second DM 7/3, Third DM 9/5

2.8 Doomsday Machines – Large (Solitaire Scenario #2)
• Deep Space markers are placed within four hexes of the player's Home System areas, except that a marker is not placed in any of the possible DM entry hexes. The player may not enter these hexes.
• The player will face a total of three DMs. Each enters at the start of the Economic Phase at the Attack Strength according to the table below.
• "#/#" – The first number is the Economic Phase the DM enters. The second number is the DM Rating.
Difficulty Table (Large):
Easy: First DM 8/5, Second DM 10/7, Third DM 12/9
Normal: First DM 8/6, Second DM 10/8, Third DM 11/10
Hard: First DM 7/5, Second DM 9/7, Third DM 11/9
Harder: First DM 7/6, Second DM 9/8, Third DM 10/10
Good Luck!: First DM 6/5, Second DM 8/7, Third DM 10/9

name: Doomsday Machines — Hidden Galactic Capitol
ref: All Good Things Solo/Co-op Scenario Book, 2.9, p.6
description: Variant for DM solitaire scenarios. An ancient Galactic Capitol is hidden among Deep Space markers.
full_text:
2.9 Doomsday Machines – Hidden GC

• Features: The GC represents an ancient, super-advanced civilization. You may not attack a GC but combat with the DM may occur in the GC hex.

• GC Discovery: The GC's location is not known at the start of the game. The GC counter is seeded into an assortment of Deep Space markers randomly placed near you.
The GC will randomly appear along the edge of your Home System markers. Set aside the GC counter when assembling the random Deep Space counters for a scenario. Also set aside 9 random Deep Space counters. The GC is added face down, at random to the separated Deep Space counters. Place these separate DS counters adjacent to your HS in hexes indicated by the blue dots. The DM does not cause these DS markers to be flipped, see 2.3.6.

• GC Trade: You may trade with the GC by extending your MS Pipelines to the GC hex as if it were one of your Colonies. Instead of gaining 1 CP for this colony, you receive 5 CP per Econ Phase.

• GC Loss Condition: If the GC is eliminated, you lose just as if your own HW had been captured or eliminated.

name: Space Amoebas (Solitaire Scenario #3)
ref: All Good Things Solo/Co-op Scenario Book, 3.0, p.6
description: Three types of Giant Space Amoebas surround your Home System. They spread during each Economic Phase and must be researched by Science Vessels before they can be attacked.
full_text:
3.0 Space Amoebas
During the game, the Space Amoebas will multiply and attempt to destroy the human player. There are similar rules for Space Amoebas included in the Competitive Scenario Book (CSB 10.0).

3.1 Setup
• There are 26 Home System markers for the player. The 30 CP Homeworld should be placed in the hex with the "H."
• Take ten of the 10 CP Deep Space Mineral markers and four of the Deep Space Barren Planets and mix them up. Place them in the 14 Deep Space hexes labeled "DS." Do not use any other Deep Space markers. Leave the other Deep Space hexes empty.
• There are three types of Giant Space Amoebas included in the game. One is placed in each hex marked with an "A."

3.2 Special Rules
• The following rules are not used: Minesweepers, Non-Player Aliens, Boarding Ships, Ground Combat, Titans, Facilities, and Ship Experience.
• The player pays no maintenance during this game.
• The Barren Planets do not have Non-Player Aliens on them.
• As in the multiplayer Space Amoeba scenarios, Minesweepers (SW) represent Science Vessels (SV), which are needed to defeat the Amoebas. Science (aka Minesweeper) technology must be researched to produce Science Vessels. Use the costs for Minesweeper ships and Minesweeper technology for producing Science Vessels.
• Researching Minesweepers (SW) instead gets the player Science Vessels (SV) which are needed to defeat the Amoebas. Actual Minesweepers are not used in this scenario.
• There are only two levels of Science technology. Ignore Minesweeper 3 technology for this scenario.

3.2.1 Victory Conditions: The human player wins by destroying all Space Amoebas.

3.3 Researching Space Amoebas
Science technology cannot be researched by the player until at least one ship has been lost to any Amoeba. The player may move a "brave" ship into an Amoeba's hex in order to find out what is coming toward their empire. Note to self: Don't ever be a Scout pilot.
• Until the player has researched each Amoeba, on a type-by-type basis, they cannot be attacked and will destroy all the player's ships (except SVs) in the same hex. If SVs are present, they may research the Amoeba before it destroys the other ships. If those SVs gather 10 or more Research Points on that Amoeba type, the other ships will not be automatically destroyed.
• Research Points carry over from turn to turn. A separate note should be kept on each type of Space Amoeba.
• Each turn an SV is in the same hex with an Amoeba, one or more dice are rolled for each SV to represent that ship's research into that type of Amoeba.
  - 1-2: Two Research Points gained.
  - 3-5: One Research Point gained.
  - 6-8: Nothing.
  - 9-10: SV destroyed.
• Level 1 Science Vessels roll one die when researching.
• Level 2 Science Vessels get to roll two dice and take the best result.
• Level 2 Science technology can't be researched until a Level 1 SV has made at least one research attempt on any one type of Amoeba.
• Each individual Amoeba does not need to be researched, but each TYPE must be. Once the player has a total of 10 Research Points on a certain TYPE of Amoeba, they may attack any Amoeba of that type for the rest of the game.
• A Science Vessel may research an Amoeba on the same turn the ship moves into its hex.
• Multiple SVs can research each turn they are in a hex with an Amoeba.
• If an SV shares a hex with an Amoeba type that has been completely researched, that ship does not participate in the combat but must still roll a die for research as normal and may be destroyed on a roll of 9-10. No further Research Points are gained.
• After 10 Research Points have been gathered on a type of Amoeba, those Amoebas will no longer automatically destroy ships. Instead, combat ensues as normal. However, Science Vessels never participate in combat with an Amoeba.

3.4 Space Amoeba Characteristics
After 10 Research Points have been gathered on a type of Amoeba, the Amoeba's characteristics are determined. Roll on the Amoeba Characteristics Table and flip all Amoebas of that type to the "researched" side. No two (or more) types of Amoebas will have the same characteristics. Reroll any repeat result.
No retrofit (technology upgrade) is needed for ships in order to attack (MRB 9.11.3).

3.5 Amoeba Characteristics Table
Roll 1: C7-1-1
Roll 2: D6-2-2
Roll 3: A7-2-2. This type of Amoeba is also vulnerable to fighters.
Roll 4: A7-2-2. This type of Amoeba becomes an A5-1-2 when faced by ONLY Raiders.
Roll 5: B5-1-2. These Amoebas have voracious appetites and get two attacks each round. Each of these attacks may lead to a Chain Attack (3.6.3).
Roll 6-7: A7-2-2. In any round in which there are 3 or more different types of ships (for example: SC, DD, and CA) attacking these Amoebas, all attacking ships get +1 to their attack.
Roll 8-10: These Amoebas can only be killed by detonating a ship inside them, no adjustment to weapons will work on this type of Amoeba. Only one ship may attempt this per turn. If the ship is a CA, BC, BB, or DN, the Amoeba is destroyed. If the ship is a DD or MB the Amoeba is destroyed on a roll of 1-8 and if the ship is a SC the Amoeba is destroyed on a roll of 1-4. In all cases, the ship attempting this is destroyed. Only the ships listed here can be used. If the Amoeba survives, other ships in the hex with the Amoeba are destroyed.

3.6 Amoeba Properties
• All Amoebas have a Tactics level of 2.
• All Amoebas are immune to Cloaking and Fighters unless otherwise specified.
• Ships never get a Fleet Size Bonus (MRB 5.1.3) against Amoebas.

3.6.1 Amoeba Target Priority: Each time an Amoeba attacks, these mindless denizens of the interstellar void roll a die to determine which ship they will attempt to swallow (attack). On a roll of 1-5 it will attack the smallest ship in the combat. On a roll of 6-10 it will attack the largest ship in the combat. Use the CP value of a ship to determine largest and smallest. Since Flagships are irreplaceable, they are considered to have the highest CP value. If there is a tie, the player chooses the target of the Space Amoeba. If a player chooses to, they may allow their SVs to be attacked.

3.6.2 Amoeba Attack Procedure: The Amoeba rolls one die and executes the attack normally, comparing its Attack Strength to the ship's Defense Strength (including technology). However, larger ships are even harder to swallow. Ships with a Hull Size of 2 get an extra +1 to their Defense Strength and ships with a Hull Size of 3 (or larger) get an extra +2 to their Defense Strength. A successful attack results in the ship being swallowed (destroyed) regardless of size. As always, a roll of a 1 is a successful attack and will swallow a ship, regardless of size.

3.6.3 Chain Attacks: If an Amoeba successfully swallows a ship, and rolls 2 or less than was needed, then the extra energy gained from swallowing the ship allows the Amoeba to immediately attack again. This can happen any number of times during a round of combat.

3.6.4 Amoeba Healing: After combat, damage to an Amoeba is healed in the same way as ships.

3.7 Spreading of Space Amoebas
Amoebas never move. Once placed, an Amoeba counter never moves to a new hex. Instead, Amoebas spread. At the start of an Economic Phase (before the player makes any purchases), each Amoeba that is adjacent to a hex without an Amoeba may spread. Place a new Amoeba of the same type in the adjacent hex.

3.7.1 Spread Procedure: The following guidelines and restrictions apply to the spreading:
• Space Amoebas begin to spread during the Economic Phase indicated on the Difficulty Table (3.9).
• Only one Amoeba may ever be in a hex.
• Spreading is automatic; however, each Amoeba may only spread one time into one hex during an Economic Phase.
• If more than one type of Amoeba is adjacent to an open hex, the human player may decide which Amoeba spreads into the hex.
• If an Amoeba may spread into more than one hex, the player chooses which hex it spreads into. However, the player must choose in such a way to allow for maximum Amoeba spreading. If another open hex is available, they cannot have an Amoeba spread into a hex that would stop a different Amoeba from spreading.
• Unlike in multiplayer Amoeba scenarios, the spreading of Amoebas is not affected by the presence or absence of planets.
• Newly placed Amoebas do not spread. New Amoebas placed that Economic Phase should be marked in some way so that they are not accidentally selected to spread.
• Because Amoebas are attracted to the energy signature of the player's Empires, they will never spread outside of the map area denoted by the chosen scenario map.
• Amoebas are not limited by the counter mix or quantity. If you run out of one type, use a counter of another type and place a numeral marker on it to show the true type of the Amoeba. Use substitutions when necessary.

3.7.2 Amoeba Contact with Ships: If there are ships in the hex in which an Amoeba spreads, a special combat round occurs immediately during the Economic Phase, with the Amoeba as the attacker. Of course, if that type of Amoeba has not been fully researched yet, it automatically destroys all ships that are not Science Vessels.

3.7.3 Amoeba Contact with System Markers: An Amoeba removes the System marker (whether explored or not) in any hex in which it spreads (only after defeating any ships present). If a hex has not been explored, the Amoeba spreads normally and the System marker is removed, sight unseen.
If a Black Hole has been revealed, an Amoeba will not spread into that hex. In all other cases, Amoebas are unaffected by System markers.

3.8 Space Amoebas and Mines
Space Amoebas are hindered from spreading by Mines. However, Mines never affect Amoebas like they do ships.

3.8.1 Procedure: If the player moves only a Mine (or a Mine and a Science Vessel) into the same hex as an Amoeba, no combat occurs. In the Economic Phase, the Mine is removed, and that Amoeba does not spread during that one Economic Phase. No other harm comes to the Amoeba.

3.8.2 Contact with Mines: Amoebas may spread onto Mines normally; in which case the Mines have no effect and are removed from the map. A Mine only stops an Amoeba from spreading if it is in the same hex as the Amoeba at the start of the Economic Phase.

3.8.3 Adapting to Mines: See CSB 10.8.3. However, when an Amoeba adapts to Mines, it only affects Amoebas of that type.

3.8.4 One Roll per Economic Phase: The roll to see if the Amoebas adapt to Mines is made only once per Amoeba type per Economic Phase, regardless of how many Amoebas were prevented from spreading.

3.9 Difficulty Table
Space Amoebas do NOT spread in the first Economic Phase. The difficulty level of the game is determined by the Economic Phase in which the Amoebas start to spread.
Easy: Amoebas start spreading in Economic Phase 7
Normal: Amoebas start spreading in Economic Phase 6
Hard: Amoebas start spreading in Economic Phase 5
Harder: Amoebas start spreading in Economic Phase 4
Good Luck!: Amoebas start spreading in Economic Phase 4* (*Both the Attack and Defense Strengths of all Amoebas are increased by 1.)

name: Solitaire Alien Empire — Non-Victory Point Rules (Scenarios #4 & #5)
ref: All Good Things Solo/Co-op Scenario Book, 4.0–4.10, p.9–14
description: One player vs 2 or 3 Alien Players. Two map layouts available (Map #1 and Map #2). APs use automated Economic Rolls to generate fleets. Only Basic Unit types.
full_text:
4.0 Solitaire Alien Empire - Non-Victory Point Rules

4.0.1 Victory Conditions: One player sets up and plays their empire as normal. Two or three Alien Players (APs) are trying to destroy the human player. The player wins by destroying the Homeworlds of all APs. The game can continue beyond Econ Phase 20. If it does, alternate the rolls on the AP Economic Sheet between rows 19 and 20.

4.0.2 Special Rules: The human player may not use Decoys. There is no need for a turn order bid - the human player always takes their turn before the APs and the APs always resolve their Economic Phases after the player. Barren Planets found in Deep Space are not inhabited by Non-Player Aliens (NPAs; MRB 18.0). In general, where the solitaire rules are silent, use common sense and play the APs intelligently.

Only Basic Units Types are used (MRB 8).
Fast Technology is not used (MRB 9.9).
Empire Advantages are not used (MRB 10).
Alien Technology cards are not used (MRB 11).
Crew Cards are not used (MRB 12).
Defense Satellite Networks are not used (MRB 14).
Additional Terrain (MRB 25) may be used.
Doomsday Machines (MRB 29) may be used.
Instant Technology Upgrades (MRB 30) may be used.
Black Hole Slingshots (MRB 31) may be used.
All other rules after MRB 18 are not used.

4.1 Alien Player Homeworlds
• APs each have a Homeworld. Use the Homeworlds and ships from the unused player colors. An AP Homeworld represents the entire empire and resources of that AP.
• Each AP starts with one Base on their Homeworld.
• APs never use MS Pipelines, Colony Ships, Shipyards, Mining Ships, or Decoys.
• An AP is eliminated when its Homeworld is eliminated. At that point it will no longer produce ships, but existing ships will continue to fight to the death.
• Until an AP launches a fleet (4.3), the only actions that need to be taken for them (other than defending against an attack) occur in the Economic Phase.
• In combat, the AP never screens ships.

4.2 AP Economic Rolls
During the Economic Phase, after the player completes their actions, each AP takes the following actions:
• APs make their Economic Roll(s). Each AP starts with one Economic Roll (Econ Roll). If the result is Economy, an Extra Economic Roll is gained every Economic Phase starting three Economic Phases later.
• If any other result is rolled, a number of CP (5, 10, or 15) is added in the appropriate column depending on difficulty. CP put in the Defense column are doubled. These columns (Fleet, Technology, Defense) carry over from turn to turn. The Defense column maxes out at 50 CP, excess is placed into Fleet.

Difficulty Table:
Easy: 2 APs, 5 CP per roll
Normal: 3 APs, 5 CP per roll
Hard: 2 APs, 10 CP per roll
Harder: 3 APs, 10 CP per roll
Really Tough: 2 APs, 15 CP per roll
Good Luck!: 3 APs, 15 CP per roll

4.3 AP Fleet Launch Rolls
When all Economic Rolls have been made, the Fleet Launch roll is made. A fleet will never launch if there is less than 6 CP in the Fleet column or if there are already 3 fleets in play for this AP.
• If AP has Fighter Tech with 27+ CP in Fleet and human has not shown equivalent PD Tech, apply -2 modifier to Fleet Launch roll.
• If AP has Cloaking Tech with 12+ CP in Fleet and human has not shown equivalent Scanner Tech, apply -2 modifier (cumulative).
• If Fleet Launch is made, determine if it is a Raider fleet (4.4). Raider fleets are built immediately. Non-Raider fleets are not built until first combat.
• All new fleets are placed on this AP's Homeworld.

4.4 AP Raider Fleets
A fleet is a Raider fleet if: AP has Cloaking technology, has at least 12 CP in Fleet column, and human has not shown equivalent Scanner technology.

4.5 AP Technology Purchasing
• APs start with Mine technology (MRB 17.1).
• AP does not attempt to purchase tech if less than 20 CP in Tech Column.
• Whenever a fleet is launched, roll for Movement tech. On 1-4, purchase next level if affordable.
• Technologies other than Movement are not purchased until combat occurs.

4.5.1 Order of Purchases: Once in combat, spend all tech CP in this order:
• Raider fleet with Cloaking 1: roll 1-6 buy Cloaking 2, 7-10 proceed to chart.
• If human used Fighters and AP has no PD, purchase PD 1.
• If human showed Mines and AP has no Minesweeper, purchase Minesweeper 1.
• If human used Raiders and AP has no adequate Scanner: roll 1-4 purchase Scanner, 5-10 skip.
• Ship Size: roll on table (SS1: Auto, SS2: 1-7, SS3: 1-6, SS4: 1-5, SS5: 1-3).
• If AP has Fighter tech and human has not shown PD: roll 1-6 buy next Fighter level.
• Remaining CP: roll on Technology Purchase Table (1-2 Attack, 3-4 Defense, 5 Tactics*, 6 Cloak**, 7 Scan, 8 Fighter, 9 PD, 10 Minesweeper).
*If Tactics rolled and AP not at Attack 2 or Defense 2, purchase lower of Attack/Defense instead.
**If human has shown Scanner 2, reroll 6s.

4.6 AP Fleet Building
After tech spent, fleet CP used to purchase ships:
• Raider fleet: all points on Raiders.
• If AP has Fighter tech and human has not shown PD: create Carrier fleet.

4.6.1 Building Priorities (non-Raider):
• LARGEST SHIP FIRST.
• POSSIBLE DESTROYER if AP has Scanner tech matching human Cloaking.
• REMAINING SHIPS: roll Fleet Composition (3 or less: Largest Fleet, 4-6: Balanced, 7-10: Largest Ships). -2 modifier if human has used Fighters and AP has PD.

4.6.2 Fleet Composition explained:
• LARGEST FLEET: maximize ship count.
• LARGEST SHIPS: maximize ship size.
• BALANCED: maximize ships using highest Attack/Defense tech.

4.6.3 No Minesweepers: AP Scouts automatically equipped with Minesweeper tech. AP never purchases Minesweeper ships.

4.7 AP Fleet Movement

4.7.1 Non-Raider Fleet Movement: Move toward Colony reachable in fewest Economic Phases. Prefer undefended Colonies (SY + single SC not considered defended). AP fleets never retreat. Cannot enter Supernovas, Cosmic Storms, or Black Holes. Stop in terrain that benefits them most. If hex has unexplored marker, do not flip it.

4.7.2 Raider Fleet Movement: Same as normal but avoid combat with larger player fleets (in CP). Never attack Colony guarded by larger force. Redirect if guard becomes equal.

4.8 AP Homeworld Defense
Defense CP not used until Homeworld attacked. AP spends all Fleet, Tech, and Defense CP to build fleets, Bases, and Mines. No Movement tech roll. Reroll 10s on Technology Purchase Table.
Defense Composition: Roll 1-3 Mines, 4-7 Balanced, 8-10 Most Bases.

4.9 Alien Player Map #1 (Solitaire Scenario #4)
26 Home System markers. Homeworld on "H." APs on "2s" (2 APs) or "2s" and "3" (3 APs). Player uses 20 CP Homeworld.

4.10 Alien Player Map #2 (Solitaire Scenario #5)
25 Home System markers (Black Hole removed). Homeworld on "X." DS markers within 7 hexes. APs on "2s" and "3." Player uses 20 CP Homeworld.

name: Using Close Encounters Rules in Solitaire Alien Empire
ref: All Good Things Solo/Co-op Scenario Book, 5.0, p.14
description: Optional overlay for Solitaire Scenarios #4 or #5 adding Close Encounters rules.
full_text:
5.0 Using Close Encounters Rules in Solitaire Alien Empire Scenarios - Non-Victory Point Rules

It is possible to play Solitaire Scenarios (4.9 or 4.10) with some of the new rules and technologies from Close Encounters. This is accomplished using the AP Flowcharts in the last pages of this scenario book and allows for the use of the following additions:
• Alien Technology cards (MRB 11.0)
• Crew cards (MRB 12.0)
• Ship Experience (MRB 37.0)
• Boarding Ships (MRB 19.0)
• Security Forces (MRB 20.0)
• Ground Combat (MRB 21.0)
• Titans (MRB 22.0)
• Resource cards (MRB 39.0)

5.1 Setup
Choose either Solitaire Scenario and set up the map as originally indicated. Use the 30 CP Homeworld instead.

5.2 Special Rules
• Victory Conditions remain the same as originally indicated.
• The AP does not use the Alien Player Victory Point Track; this mechanic is introduced in section 6.3.3.
• The AP only builds either regular fleets or Raider fleets as in the original game.
• Like the human player, the AP may now use the features listed above except Alien Technology cards, which they cannot acquire.
• Use the original "Alien Economic Sheet" on page 56.
• A Colony captured by the AP cannot be bombarded by the human player. The AP will defend captured Colonies with Mines, Bases, and Ground Units.
• APs may gain technology by capturing a player Colony (MRB 21.13.5; MRB 21.13.6). Human player may steal technology from AP by capturing AP Homeworld with Ground Units.
• Facilities (MRB 36.0) are not used.
• Empire Advantages (MRB 10.0) may not be used.
• A Flagship (MRB 23.0) may be used by the human player only.
• If human player colonizes a Deep Space Barren Planet, they may gain an Alien Technology card as per MRB 11.1.

5.3 Alien Player Behavior
Follow the AP flowcharts starting on page 57, with exceptions:
• Econ Roll may result in Extra Econ as per original "Alien Economic Sheet" (4.2).
• AP does not colonize Barren Planets.
• AP does not have a Bank (6.5.2).
• AP does not launch Expansion or Extermination fleets. Non-Raider fleets treated as regular fleets.
• Regular AP fleets always target the nearest, least defended Colony.
• During ship purchasing, follow the branch for Carriers, Raiders, Expansion fleets and Ground Combat units. Ignore Extermination branch. All Regular AP fleets get a fully loaded Transport for free.
• Ignore items specific to Victory Point scenarios (6.0) including Bank, AP movement, exploration and colonizing rules.

name: Solitaire Alien Empire — Victory Point Rules (Scenario #6)
ref: All Good Things Solo/Co-op Scenario Book, 6.0, p.15
description: Advanced solitaire scenario with Galactic Capitol, Warp Points, Victory Points tracking, and Black Hole Jumping technology.
full_text:
6.0 Solitaire Alien Empire - Victory Point Rules

Evil alien races are trying to eXplore, eXpand, eXploit, and eXterminate your end of the galaxy and must be stopped!

Note: This scenario assumes a good understanding of the solitaire rules in section 4.0.

6.1 Setup

6.1.1 Homeworlds: 30 CP Homeworld on the "X." "AP" marks the two Alien Player Homeworlds.

6.1.2 Starting Tech: Player starts with Terraforming 1 and Exploration 1.

6.1.3 Galactic Capitol: Ancient GC in the center surrounded by six Nebulae. Place a 5 CP Colony marker on it. Generates militia if attacked by AP Ground Units. Grows back up to 5 CP during Economic Phase. Player cannot bombard, capture, or deliver Minerals to it, but may place Ground Units to defend.

6.1.4 Galactic Minerals: 10-Mineral counter at GC. Respawns when consumed.

6.1.5 Warp Points: WP at GC (no counter) plus WP on each side of player's Home System. All connected to GC.

6.1.6 Deep Space Markers: Placed everywhere above the black line except around GC, player's HS, and WP spaces.

6.1.7 Alien Planets: 30 CP Homeworld markers on "AP" spaces.

6.1.8 Starting CP: Each AP starts with 100 CP in Expansion/Defense Bank.

6.2 AP Difficulty - VP Rules:
Easy: 5 CP/Econ Roll, Bank 100, Max Defense 50
Normal: 10 CP/Econ Roll, Bank 100, Max Defense 50
Hard: 15 CP/Econ Roll, Bank 100, Max Defense 50

6.3 Victory Conditions

6.3.1 Alien Victory: Destroy GC, destroy player's Homeworld, or accumulate 10 VPs.

6.3.2 Human Victory: Destroy both AP Homeworlds.

6.3.3 Tracking VPs: Shared VP total. Always 10 for AP victory.
AP colonizes/captures DS planet: +2 VPs
AP destroys/captures player Colony: +2 VPs
Player destroys/(re)captures AP Colony: -2 VPs
Alien Technology cards in AP possession: +1 VP each

6.3.4 Game Length: Can continue beyond Econ Phase 20.

6.4 Special Rules
Ship Experience (MRB 37.0), NPAs (MRB 18.0), Transports/Ground Combat (MRB 21.0) used. Human may use Replicators expansion technologies.

6.4.1 Prohibited: Empire Advantages, Crew cards, Facilities, Resource cards. APs don't use Replicators technologies.

6.4.2 Black Hole Jumping: 2 levels (20 CP / 40 CP). Level 1: enter Black Holes without survival roll. Level 2: treat all face-up Black Holes and Warp Points as connected. Hard difficulty requires Movement 3 first.

6.4.3 Alien Starting Tech: Terraforming 1, Exploration 1, Ground Combat 1.

6.4.4 Alien Colonies: APs can capture or build Colonies.

6.4.5 Attacking Alien Colonies: Player may attack/capture AP Colonies. Destroyed/captured = -2 VPs.

6.4.6 APs Gaining Alien Tech Cards: If AP captures NPA planet, draw Alien Tech card. Cannot use effects but each card = +1 VP.

6.4.7 Stealing Alien Tech Cards: Human can steal (random) from AP by capturing AP Colony in DS with Ground Units.

6.4.8 Capturing AP Technology: Player gains tech by capturing AP Colonies (MRB 21.13.6). Treated as DS Colonies.

6.4.9 Eliminating AP Homeworlds: Eliminated AP continues acting. Units not removed. Cannot gain VP/cards but can still cause loss.

6.5 Alien Player Behavior

6.5.1 Fleet Types: Extermination (6.6), Expansion (6.7), or Raider (6.9). Max 3 fleets per AP.

6.5.2 Bank and Defense Limits: Expansion fleet adds 50 CP from Bank. Defense maxes at 50 CP. Reroll Defense results at max.

6.5.3 Economic Phase: After player.

6.5.4 Shared Intelligence: APs share all info.

6.5.5 Resource Gathering: Extermination/Expansion fleets collect Minerals for 1 extra Econ Roll next phase. Raiders cannot.

6.5.6 Space Wrecks: Destroyed immediately. No VPs.

6.5.7 Colonization: AP may invade or build Colony Ship (6.8.4). Each producing AP Colony gives 1 extra Econ Roll.

6.5.8 Blockaded AP Homeworlds: Continue Economic Phases but no fleet launch. All CP to Defense/Bank. Next Movement Phase launches Defense fleet.

6.5.9 Combat: Never retreats. Positions defensively if out of range.

6.5.10 Screening: As a human player would.

6.5.11 Captured Ships: Cannot be scrapped for tech.

6.5.12 Capturing Player Tech: Gains tech as in competitive game (MRB 21.13). Also gains 10 CP in Tech column.

6.5.13 Experience: Tracked normally (MRB 37.0).

6.5.14 Minesweepers: AP Scouts equip Minesweeper tech. No Minesweeper ships.

6.5.15 Regional Map/Space Pirates: No effect on AP. Discard.

6.5.16 AP avoids Cosmic Storms if possible. Moves through Folds normally.

6.6 Extermination Fleets
6.6.1 Targeting: Random - player's Homeworld or GC.
6.6.2 Movement: Beeline to destination, avoiding combat and other Colonies if able.

6.7 Expansion Fleets
6.7.1 Targeting: Closest NPA planet or player Colony. If equidistant, choose one enabling more DS exploration.
6.7.2 Movement: Toward target, avoiding Homeworlds and GC. Destinations reset each Movement Phase. Remainder stays and defends permanently (doesn't count for 3 fleet limit).

6.8 Non-Raider Fleet Movement and Exploration
6.8.1 Movement: Hindered by terrain like human fleets.
6.8.2 Exploration: Equivalent of one Cruiser with Exploration 1. One DS marker per fleet per turn.
6.8.3 Barren Planets: Place 4 random NPA counters. Combat occurs.
6.8.4 Colonizing: If AP won space combat, Colony Ship + 2d10 CP escorts placed at nearest AP Colony/Homeworld at no cost.

6.9 Raider Fleets
As in the base game, the AP may launch a Raider fleet instead of a normal fleet. These fleets always target the nearest, least defended player Colony. If 3 fleets already in play for this AP, launch is cancelled.

6.9.1 Non-Player Aliens: Raider fleets will remain cloaked when in the same hex as an NPA planet.

6.9.2 May Not Capture Colonies: Unlike Expansion and Extermination fleets, Raider fleets have no chance of building Ground Units to capture a Colony. They may still destroy a Colony and trigger the construction of a Colony Ship (6.8.4).

6.9.3 Deep Space Markers: Unlike Expansion and Extermination fleets, Raider fleets are unaffected by unrevealed Deep Space markers and do not cause them to become flipped. A player may not enter a hex with an unrevealed Deep Space marker that is occupied by a Raider fleet. A player may not use Exploration technology on an unrevealed Deep Space marker in the same hex as a Raider fleet.

6.9.4 Asteroids and Nebulae: Raider fleets are affected by these terrain types just like human fleets. They will avoid Nebulae if able as long as doing so does not change the number of Economic Phases it takes to reach their destination.

6.9.5 Avoiding Combat: Raiders will avoid combat with player fleets if the player fleet has greater CP than the Raider fleet. They also avoid combat with a fleet containing a Flagship if the rest of the Flagship's fleet CP cost is greater than half the Raider fleet's CP.

name: Replicator Solitaire (Scenarios #7 & #8)
ref: All Good Things Solo/Co-op Scenario Book, 7.0–7.14, p.19–28
description: Solitaire scenario against the Replicators. Two map sizes: Standard (7.13) and Large (7.14). Extensive autonomous Replicator AI rules for exploration, fleet formation, movement, combat, and production.
full_text:
7.0 Replicator Solitaire
This solitaire scenario against the Replicators includes several flowcharts for reference on pages 62-67.

7.1 Map Setup
• Use either the Normal (7.13) or Large (7.14) Replicator Solitaire map.
• Place each Homeworld anywhere in the Home System row furthest from the opponent.

7.2 Starting Forces & Technology

7.2.1 Human Player: 30 CP Homeworld, 4 Colony Ships, 4 Shipyards, 3 Scouts, 1 Miner, 1 Flagship (optional).

7.2.2 Replicators: Full Homeworld, 6 Type 0 ships, 1 Flagship (optional), Point Defense, Scanners, Minesweepers, Move 2 (Move 3 with Fast Replicators advantage).

7.3 Special Rules

7.3.1 Difficulty Level:
Easy: Player starts with two additional Colony Ships.
Normal: No changes.
Hard: Replicators start with two additional Hull Points and one free RP.
Impossible: Replicators start with three additional Hull Points and two free RPs.

7.3.2 Hulls: "Hull Size" and "Hull Points" are equivalent when comparing.

7.3.3 Excluded Rules (*never used in any Replicators scenario):
*Facilities, *Terraforming 2, *Unique Ships, *Experience, *Reaction Movement, *Space Amoebas, *Blood Brothers, Decoys, Resource Cards, *Galactic Capitol, Regional Maps, Space Pirates, Warp Points, Doomsday Machines.

7.3.4 NPAs and Alien Tech: Barren Planets have NPAs (MRB 18.0). Remove MINESWEEP JAMMER from Alien Tech deck.

7.3.5 Empire Advantages: Remove WARP GATES Replicator EA. Deal 2 EA + 1 Replicator EA. After player selects, reveal Replicator EA.

7.3.6 Initiative: Replicators always have Initiative unless human spends 15 CP. Replicators never spend CP on Initiative.

7.3.7 Replicator Ship Behavior: Facedown = Hulls. Flagship and Exploration ships always faceup. Ships entering Replicator Colony convert back to Hulls.

7.3.8 Miscellaneous: No hidden information. Roll die for unclear choices.

7.4 Exploration Procedures
After Replicator HS fully explored, Replicators receive Exploration tech (free) and 3 Exploration ships placed at the 3 Full Colonies closest to unexplored DS hexes. No Replicator ship ever enters unexplored DS hex. Exploration ships use Exploration tech only.

7.4.1 Flagship Exploration: Replicator Flagship does not equip Exploration tech.

7.5 Exploration Ships
One explores toward human player HS, two explore DS near Replicator HS.

7.5.1 Exploring Toward Human Player: Creates direct explored path.
7.5.2 Exploring Nearby DS: Row by row from Replicator HS.
7.5.3 Priorities: Closest unexplored DS marker. Ties broken by proximity to explored hexes, then distance from Replicator HS.
7.5.4 Additional Rules: Never join Fleets. Never enter hex with human ships. Always retreat. If all eliminated, create replacement. After DS fully explored, convert to 2 Hulls.

7.6 Replicator Movement
Never enter Black Holes or Supernovas. Prefer converting to Hulls via Replicator Colony. Prioritize hexes with unescorted non-combat ships and Asteroids/Nebulae.

7.6.1 During HS Exploration: Move into nearest unexplored HS hex. Minerals absorbed, planets colonized immediately.
7.6.2 HS Fully Explored: All ships (except Exploration) form/join Fleets.

7.7 Fleet Formation
Fleets represented by Fleet markers. Multiple ships in a hex does NOT make a Fleet. Fleets never split (except Tasks). Only combine to attack on same turn.

7.7.1 Forming Fleets: Ships move toward nearest Fleet and join.
7.7.2 The First Fleet: Forms when HS fully explored. Follows the human-bound Exploration ship.
7.7.3 Additional Fleets: Form when: (a) separate explored path to human HS exists (not within 4 hexes of another Fleet), or (b) human ship closer to Replicator Colony than any Fleet.

7.8 Fleet Movement
Steps: 1. Move Exploration ships. 2. Form additional Fleets. 3. Move Fleets. 4. Move non-Fleet ships toward Fleets.

7.8.1 Movement Priorities:
1. Attack a Colony (undefended or Equal/Inferior guard).
2. Attack an Inferior Fleet (limited by Self-Preservation).
3. Attack an Equal Fleet if it could attack Replicator Colony.
4. Attack NPA planet with 12+ Hull Points (limited by Self-Preservation).
5. Move toward/away from human Colony or ships via explored path (limited by Self-Preservation).

7.8.2 Fleet Comparison: Total Hull Points each side. Ignore non-combat/Exploration ships. Human Base = double (6 HP). Mine = 1.5 HP. 25%+ more = Superior, other = Inferior. Otherwise Equal.

7.8.3 Self-Preservation: Priorities 2, 4, 5 limited. Replicators won't move where a Superior fleet could attack them. Never fall back beyond Homeworld. Replicators ignore Black Hole Slingshot possibility when applying Self-Preservation.

7.8.4 Replicator Fleet Movement Examples: Human player has two Destroyers (2 HP) in one hex and three Destroyers (3 HP) in an adjacent hex. Replicators have a Fleet of three Hulls two hexes away from both. No Colony can be attacked (Priority 1).

• If Fleet can move 2 hexes: attacks the 2-DD fleet (Priority 2). Won't attack the 3-DD fleet (not Inferior). Post-battle losses becoming Inferior to the 3-DD fleet is not considered.

• If Fleet can move 2 hexes AND one more Hull can also reach: Hull combines with Fleet, both human fleets now Inferior, so Replicators attack the 3-DD fleet (strongest Inferior, Priority 2).

• If Fleet can only move 1 hex: won't move into any hex where both human fleets could attack together. 5 DDs Superior to 3 Hulls. Priority 5 limited by Self-Preservation.

• If Replicators move 1 hex but human moves 2: Fleet retreats to a hex at least 3 hexes from one human fleet (Self-Preservation). May retreat toward Replicator HS. If Asteroid hex is adjacent and human can't enter it, Fleet enters it to stay close without being attacked.

• If both move 1 hex near human HS: Fleet moves to hex closest to a human Colony (Priority 5) where it can't be attacked by both human fleets together.

7.9 Replicator Tasks
7.9.1 Gain NPA RP: Assign nearest ship to attack NPA planet.
7.9.2 Gain Human RP: If attack would earn RP, assign one ship.
7.9.3 Colonize Undefended Planet: Assign ship to colonize.
7.9.4 Conquer NPA Planet: Only on Large map, before Econ Phase 10, with 10+ ships near HS.

7.10 Colonization: Always expend one Hull Point only.

7.11 Combat

7.11.1 Converting Hulls to Ships: Follow steps in order:
7.11.1.1 Unescorted Non-Combat: Destroyed immediately.
7.11.1.2 Lone SC/DD/Transport: Roll 1-5 combat, 6-10 eliminate without conversion.
7.11.1.3 Scan Ships: Based on Raider presence (die roll tables).
7.11.1.4 Minesweepers: Based on Mine presence (die roll tables).
7.11.1.5 Point Defense: Based on Fighter presence (die roll tables).
7.11.1.6 Remaining: Convert to highest RP ships possible.

7.11.1.7 Conversion Example: The Replicators have 5 RP and are attacking. They have one SW, one PD, two Type II ships, and 10 unrevealed Hulls. The human player has seven Mines, three Fighters, one CV, and two DDs. There is a Raider within three hexes.

Steps 7.11.1.1 & 7.11.1.2 are skipped (no unescorted non-combat, no lone SD/DD/Transport).

7.11.1.3: Raiders encountered before + Raider within 3 hexes = third row. Roll 9 = two Hulls convert to Scan ships. 8 unrevealed Hulls remain.

7.11.1.4: Mines in battle + encountered before = last row. Roll 2 = enough SW to clear half Mines. They have Minesweeping 2 (swept 3 Mines before). One SW already present, convert one Hull to add another SW = clear 4 Mines. 7 unrevealed Hulls remain.

7.11.1.5: Fighters in battle + encountered before = fifth row. Roll 1 = one PD hull, but PD already present, so no additional PD.

7.11.1.6: 7 remaining Hulls convert to three Type V ships and one Type IV ship.

Resulting Fleet: one Scan, two SWs, one PD, two Type II, one Type IV, three Type V.

7.11.2 Attack Priorities: PD fires at Fighters. Scan fires at Raiders. Others: smallest Hull, lowest Defense, highest Attack, unfired.

7.11.3 Screening: Replicators never screen. Scan/SW ships only targetable if human has more ships.

7.11.4 Retreat: If human has 3x Hull Points. Flagship retreats at 2 damage.

7.12 Economic Phase
Each Full Colony produces one Hull. Can carry over 30+ CP.

7.12.1 Production: Econ 1-3: Buy extra Hull at HW for 8 CP.
Econ 4+: Based on Empire Advantage or die roll (1-4: Hull 8 CP, 5-8: RP 30 CP, 9-10: Move tech 20 CP).

7.13 Replicators Standard: Standard-size map.

7.14 Replicators Large: Larger map with more Deep Space rows.

name: Doomsday Machine Coop (2-Player) — Victory Point Rules
ref: All Good Things Solo/Co-op Scenario Book, 8.0, p.28
description: Two empires cooperate against incoming Doomsday Machines with Galactic Capitol and Victory Point tracking.
full_text:
8.0 Doomsday Machine Coop (2-Player) - Victory Point Rules
An evil race is sending DMs at two cooperating empires.

8.1 Setup

8.1.1 Homeworlds: Players place Homeworlds on "H" hexes. 30 CP Homeworld used.

8.1.2 Starting Tech: Players start with Terraforming 1 and Exploration 1.

8.1.3 Turn Order: DM "team" always goes first unless a player spends 5 CP in the bid (MRB 7.4). Only players that paid 5 CP may move before DMs (see 1.1.10).

8.1.4 Special Rules: Alien Technology cards (MRB 11.0), Non-Player Aliens (MRB 18.0), and Ground Units (MRB 21.0) may be used. NOT used: Empire Advantages (MRB 10.0), Crew cards (MRB 12.0), Facilities (MRB 36.0), Ship Experience (MRB 37.0), Resource cards (MRB 39.0).

8.1.5 Galactic Capitol: Ancient GC in center surrounded by six Nebulae. Place 5 CP Colony marker. Neither player may bombard, capture, or deliver Minerals to GC, but may defend it from DM attack.

8.1.6 Galactic Minerals: 10-Mineral counter at GC. Respawns when consumed. Players split Minerals.

8.1.7 Warp Points: WP at GC (no counter). WP1 counter on "WP" space between players. Both players may use at any time.

8.1.8 Deep Space Markers: Placed everywhere above black line except around GC, in player HS, and WP spaces.

8.2 Victory Conditions

8.2.1 Player Victory: Survive the DM onslaught.

8.2.2 DM Victory: Either player loses Homeworld, GC destroyed, or DMs accumulate 10 VPs.

8.2.3 Tracking DM VPs:
Destroyed Player Colony: +2 VPs
Destroyed NPA Planet: +2 VPs
Player colonizes/captures NPA Colony: -2 VPs

8.2.4 Deep Space Colonization: Each DS planet colonized by human = -2 VPs.

8.3 Doomsday Machine Details
Where silent, DMs operate as in solitaire (2.0).

8.3.1 Arrival Times: Per Difficulty Table (8.7) after players complete Economic Phases.

8.3.2 Determine Type: Roll 1-6 Extermination DM, 7-10 Exploration DM.
Extermination DMs target Homeworlds/GC. Exploration DMs target nearest Colony/NPA planet (excluding GC).

8.3.3 Arrival Locations: Roll 1-4 Left, 5-6 Center, 7-10 Right.

8.3.4 Strength/Weakness: Strength from DM Strength Table (8.5). Weakness from DM Weakness Table (8.6) on first encounter.

8.3.5 Movement Rate: 1 hex/turn. No Warp Points. Unexplored hexes immediately revealed.

8.3.6 Folds/Regional Maps/Space Pirates: DMs move through Folds normally. Regional Maps and Pirates removed.

8.3.7 Danger/Lost in Space/Supernovas/Black Holes: No effect. Removed immediately.

8.3.8 Asteroids/Nebulae/Quasars/Cosmic Storms: No effect in combat. DMs don't avoid them. Don't destroy Asteroids.

8.3.9 Minerals: Removed from game.

8.3.10 Space Wrecks: Destroyed immediately. No VP.

8.3.11 Planets and Colonies: DM destroys planet after defeating defenders. Replace with Asteroid counter. +2 VPs.

8.3.12 DS Barren Planets: Place 4 random NPAs. NPAs fight DM. Planet destroyed after = +2 VPs.

8.3.13 Combat Behavior: Always initiate combat. Target most threatening ships first.

8.3.14 Damage/Healing: Carries over. +2 HP upon destroying planet.

8.3.15 Extermination DMs: Left side targets leftmost player HW. Right targets rightmost. Center targets GC.

8.3.16 Exploration DMs: Target closest NPA planet or player Colony. Avoid HW and GC. Recheck targets each Economic Phase.

8.4 Black Hole Jumping: As per 6.4.2 with WPs as per 6.1.5.

8.5 DM Strength Table: Same as solitaire (2.5).

8.6 DM Weakness Table: Same as solitaire (2.6).

8.7 DM Difficulty (2-Player):
Easy: 6/1, 7/3, 8/5, 10/7, 12/9
Normal: 6/3, 7/4, 8/6, 10/8, 12/10
Hard: 5/4, 6/5, 7/7, 9/9, 10/10
Harder: 5/5, 6/6, 7/8, 8/10, 10/8x2
Good Luck!: 5/6, 6/7, 7/9, 8/8x2, 10/9x3
"#/#" = Economic Phase/DM Rating. "x2"/"x3" = multiple DMs launched simultaneously.

name: Doomsday Machine Coop — Hidden Galactic Capitol Variant
ref: All Good Things Solo/Co-op Scenario Book, 8.1.9, p.29
description: Variant for all DM coop scenarios. The GC counter is shuffled with random Deep Space counters.
full_text:
8.1.9 Hidden Galactic Capitol - DM Scenario Variant
Setup as per 2.9 shuffling the GC counter in with DS counters and placing in the blue dot hexes. Place the WP in any non-blue dot hex adjacent to one of the player home systems. Instead of using the table in 8.3.3 for DM entry, roll 1d10 and place the DM on the hex matching the result. All other setup is per the above scenario.

name: Doomsday Machine Coop (3-Player) — Victory Point Rules
ref: All Good Things Solo/Co-op Scenario Book, 9.0, p.31
description: 3-player version of the DM Coop scenario. Same rules as 2-player with adjusted map and DM targeting.
full_text:
9.0 Doomsday Machine Coop (3-Player) - Victory Point Rules

9.1 Setup
The gameplay in this scenario is the same as in the 2-Player Doomsday Machine Coop scenario except as noted below.

9.1.1 Deep Space Markers: Deep Space markers are placed on all white hexes that are not otherwise marked.

9.2 Doomsday Machine Destinations

9.2.1 Extermination DMs: Target the player Homeworld on the opposite side of the board from their starting position. They avoid the Galactic Capitol hex at all costs.

9.2.2 Exploration DMs: Target whichever is nearest - a player Colony, a Non-Player Alien planet, or the Galactic Capitol.

9.3 Victory Conditions: Same as 2-player scenario.

DM Difficulty (3-Player):
Easy: 6/1, 7/3, 8/5, 10/7, 12/9
Normal: 6/3, 7/4, 8/6, 10/8, 12/10
Hard: 5/4, 6/5, 7/7, 9/9, 10/10
Harder: 5/5, 6/6, 7/8, 8/10, 10/8x2
Good Luck!: 5/6, 6/7, 7/9, 8/8x2, 10/9x3

name: Doomsday Machine Coop (4-Player) — Victory Point Rules
ref: All Good Things Solo/Co-op Scenario Book, 10.0, p.31
description: 4-player version of the DM Coop scenario. GC surrounded by 3 Nebulae. Adjusted DM targeting.
full_text:
10.0 Doomsday Machine Coop (4-Player) - Victory Point Rules

10.1 Setup
Same as 2-Player except as noted below.

10.1.1 Deep Space Markers: Placed on all hexes not otherwise marked.

10.1.2 Galactic Capitol: Placed as indicated, surrounded by three Nebulae.

10.2 Doomsday Machine Destinations

10.2.1 Extermination DMs: Left/right DMs target opposite side player with least CP income. Center DMs target player with least CP income overall. Ties chosen randomly. Avoid GC at all costs.

10.2.2 Exploration DMs: Target the Galactic Capitol. Players 1 and 2 particularly in danger from left/right Exploration DMs.

10.3 DM Difficulty (4-Player):
Easy: 6/2, 7/4, 8/7, 11/9, 12/7x2
Normal: 6/3, 7/6, 8/9, 9/10, 12/9x2
Hard: 5/3, 6/5, 7/7, 9/8x2, 10/10x2
Harder: 5/4, 6/6, 7/6x2, 10/8x2, 12/9x3
Good Luck!: 5/4x2, 6/6, 7/6x2, 9/8x2, 11/10x3

10.4 Victory Conditions: Same as 2-player scenario.

name: Alien Empire Coop (2-Player) — Victory Point Rules
ref: All Good Things Solo/Co-op Scenario Book, 11.0, p.32
description: 2-player cooperative version of the Solitaire Alien Empire VP scenario.
full_text:
11.0 Alien Empire Coop (2-Player) - Victory Point Rules

11.1 Setup
Same as solitaire section 6.0 except as noted.

11.1.1 Extermination Fleet Targeting: If launched during an odd numbered Economic Phase, target is the Galactic Capitol. Otherwise, target nearest player Homeworld.

11.2 AP Difficulty - 2-Player COOP:
Easy: 2 APs, 5 CP/Econ, Starting Bank 150, Max Defense 50
Normal: 2 APs, 10 CP/Econ, Starting Bank 150, Max Defense 50
Hard: 2 APs, 15 CP/Econ, Starting Bank 150, Max Defense 50

11.3 Victory Conditions: AP wins if 10 VP collected.

name: Alien Empire Coop (3-Player) — Victory Point Rules
ref: All Good Things Solo/Co-op Scenario Book, 12.0, p.32
description: 3-player cooperative version of the Alien Empire VP scenario.
full_text:
12.0 Alien Empire Coop (3-Player) - Victory Point Rules

12.1 Setup
Same as solitaire section 6.0 except as noted.

12.1.1 Starting Home Systems: Most experienced player uses Player 1 area.

12.1.2 Deep Space Markers: Placed on every white hex not otherwise marked.

12.1.3 Aliens: "AP" marks Homeworlds. Two APs represented by one color but treated as separate empires. Use spare counters as needed.

12.1.4 Extermination Fleet Targeting: Odd Economic Phase targets GC. Otherwise targets Homeworld of player with least CP income.

12.1.5 Expansion/Raider Fleets: Must spend at least one Economic Phase in Deep Space before entering a player's Home System.

12.2 AP Difficulty - 3-Player COOP:
Easy: 2 APs, 10 CP/Econ, Starting Bank 200, Max Defense 50
Normal: 2 APs, 10 CP/Econ, Starting Bank 200, Max Defense 50
Hard: 2 APs, 20 CP/Econ, Starting Bank 200, Max Defense 50

12.3 Victory Conditions: AP wins if 10 VP collected.

name: AP Bot
ref: All Good Things Solo/Co-op Scenario Book, 13.0, p.33
description: New mode replacing the automated Alien Player with a more sophisticated bot that emulates a human player. Can substitute in almost any scenario.
full_text:
13.0 AP Bot

13.1 Purpose
New mode making the AP act more like a player substitute or "bot". Can be used in almost any previously published scenario. Treat this set of rules as completely different from the solo/coop scenarios above. The flowcharts at the end of this scenario book do NOT apply to this iteration of the AP Bot.

13.2 Definitions
• 1d100: Two d10 generating 1-100.
• AP/AP bot: Used interchangeably.
• EV: Exploration Vessel. FOB: Forward Operating Base. HS: Home System.
• Known AP Fleet: Technology tracked on spare Production Sheet. Can become Unknown again.
• Map Size: Number of DS hexes on shortest route between AP and nearest Player HS at game start.
• Player: Human player. SCT: Special Counter Tech.
• Unknown AP Fleet: Face down fleet with unknown units/technology.

13.3 Prime Directive
Where the rules are silent or conflict, use common sense and play your opponent intelligently. This rule supersedes all other rulings. Do not alter the AP's Strategy after employing the Prime Directive.

13.4 Scenarios
The revised AP can substitute for a human player in most scenarios (e.g. CSB 2.2 2-Player Standard).

13.4.1 Expansion Goals Table:
Map Size <2: 0 DS Colonies, 1 EV, 0 Max Terraform, Move Roll 2
Map Size 2: 0 DS Colonies, 1 EV, 0 Max Terraform, Move Roll 3
Map Size 4: 1 DS Colony, 2 EVs, 1 Max Terraform, Move Roll 5
Map Size 6: 2 DS Colonies, 3 EVs, 2 Max Terraform, Move Roll 7
Map Size 8: 2 DS Colonies, 3 EVs, 2 Max Terraform, Move Roll 9
Map Size >8: 3 DS Colonies, 4 EVs, 2 Max Terraform, Move Roll 9

13.5 First Player: Decided randomly (MRB 3.0). AP always resolves Econ Phase after players.

13.6 New Hidden Galactic Capitol Rules: See separate entry.

13.7 Lost in Space Resolution: See 1.1.9.

13.8 Regional Map Resolution: For AP or players, flip and resolve all eligible adjacent hexes as if flipped by Exploration technology.

13.9 Player Alliance Rules: Players need not use alliance rules. AP treats all Players as if allied.

13.10 AP Alliances: APs have UNEASY ALLIANCE but don't share information. Operate completely separately.

13.11 Victory Points: AP scenarios no longer use VPs. Colony Point rule (CSB 1.1.7) may be used instead.

13.12 Galactic Capitol: If used, AP may randomly target GC as another HW for Extermination. If GC captured/eliminated, AP wins.

13.13 Number of Alien Players: Should generally equal number of human players. For extra challenge, add one AP and decrease Difficulty by one step.

name: AP Bot — Hidden Galactic Capitol Rules
ref: All Good Things Solo/Co-op Scenario Book, 13.6, p.34
description: Variant for the AP Bot adding a hidden Galactic Capitol.
full_text:
13.6 New Hidden Galactic Capitol Rules
• These rules represent a new scenario where the GC is hidden at the start. The AP Bot may be used with almost any previous scenario and does not require this scenario.
• Features: The GC represents an ancient, super-advanced civilization. It has a 5 CP colony counter on it. It will generate Militia if attacked and will lose levels if bombarded like a Colony. It will also grow a level at the end of every Econ Phase if bombarded below the 5 CP level. Human players may not attack a GC but combat with the AP may occur in the GC hex. Human players may place ground troops on the GC which will fight alongside the GC Militia to defend against AP invasion.
• GC Discovery: The GC's location is not known at the start of the game. The GC counter is seeded into an assortment of Deep Space markers randomly placed near the human players. Set aside the GC counter and 9 random Deep Space counters. The GC is added face down at random. If the scenario has fewer than 9 DS border hexes, remove random non-GC counters to match.
• Place these DS counters adjacent to your HS. If playing with more than 1 human player, distribute them equidistant from each HS edge. If solitaire, place in the first row of DS hexes centering to your HW.
• GC Trade: Extend MS Pipelines to GC hex for 5 CP per Econ Phase (instead of 1 CP). Multiple human players may benefit simultaneously. Optionally replace economic gain in other GC scenarios with this rule.
• GC Loss Condition: If the GC is captured or eliminated, the Players lose.
• Deep Space DMs: Never attack the Galactic Capitol.
